voices.html: All the life or health which is diffused through the body must flow to us from Christ. As the root conveys sap to the whole tree, so all the vigor we possess must flow from Christ. Each member makes its contribution to the whole, and all increase tends to exalt more highly the glory of Christ from whom all proceeds.

The word rendered fitly joined together is the same used in Ephesians 2:21 with more technical accuracy, of a building clamped or bonded together. Here the two words are applied to the union of the limbs of the body as being jointed and brought into close contact, so that all life flows from the Head.

From which: out of which as the source of energy and direction. Through every joint of the supply: through every joint that contributes to the supply. In due measure: the assumption that each part of the body works proportionately as it ought. In love: the sphere and atmosphere of the whole growth.

17.synthesis: With full apostolic solemnity Paul turns to the contrast between the Christian life and the life of the Gentile world. Calvin describes this as Paul explaining what fruits the doctrine of the gospel ought to yield, beginning with the exhortation to renounce the way of unbelievers and arguing from its inconsistency with their present views. Barnes notes that Paul bears witness in the name of the Lord Jesus, not as expressing his own private opinion but with full authority, reminding them of what they were before conversion and of the manner in which the surrounding heathen still lived. Wesley connects this verse back to verse 1: Paul returns to where he began, testifying in the Lord's name. Clarke enumerates the particulars of the Gentile condition Paul will describe: walking in vanity of mind, which is the root of all evil walking, the loss of the knowledge of the true God leading inevitably to practical darkness and corruption. Robertson notes the infinite present active of peripatein in indirect command, placing the prohibition under apostolic authority.

voices.html: He next inquires what fruits the doctrine of the gospel ought to yield in the lives of Christians, or begins to explain minutely the nature of that edification by which doctrine ought to be followed. He first exhorts them to renounce the way of unbelievers, arguing from its inconsistency with their present views and calling.

I bear witness in the name of the Lord Jesus, or ministering by his authority. The object is to exhort them to walk worthy of their high calling and to adorn the doctrine of the Saviour. With this view, he reminds them of what they were before they were converted and of the manner in which the heathen around them lived.

He returns thither where he began, Ephesians 4:1, and testifies in the Lord, in the name and by the authority of the Lord Jesus. In the vanity of their mind: having lost the knowledge of the true God, Romans 1:21. This is the root of all evil walking.

18.synthesis: The Gentiles are darkened in their understanding, alienated from the life of God through the ignorance in them, because of the hardness of their hearts. Calvin observes that the phrase life of God may mean either what is accounted life in God's sight or the life God bestows on the elect by the Spirit of regeneration; in both cases the meaning is the same: our ordinary life is nothing more than an empty image of life. Barnes gives the social observation: the understanding becomes darkened by indulgence in sin, a well-known fact seen as much in the present as in Paul's day; a man who yields habitually to any sin finds his moral perceptions becoming confused. Wesley states the sequence with precision: having their understanding darkened through ignorance, so that they are totally void of the light of God, being alienated from the divine and spiritual life through the hardness of heart that is both cause and effect of the alienation. Ellicott notes that Paul dwells with intense emphasis on the moral rather than the intellectual dimension of this darkness.

voices.html: Our ordinary life, as men, is nothing more than an empty image of life, not only because it quickly passes, but also because, while we live, our souls, not keeping close to God, are dead. The understanding becomes darkened through the ignoring of God, and from this darkened understanding flow all the moral corruptions Paul is about to describe.

The understanding becomes darkened by indulgence in sin; a fact seen now as well as then. A man who yields habitually to any sin finds his moral perceptions becoming confused and ultimately destroyed. The darkness of the Gentiles was not merely intellectual but moral; it was the darkness of alienation from the life of God.

The phrase the life of God is unique. It may be interpreted as the life given by God, the life eternal of John 17:3. St. Paul, passing lightly over the intellectual loss, dwells on the moral with intense and terrible emphasis: they have lost the capacity of moral pain, the natural and healthful consequence of sin against our true natures.

19.synthesis: Being past feeling, they gave themselves over to lasciviousness, to work all uncleanness with greediness. Calvin describes the worst of all evils: having destroyed the sensibilities of the heart and allayed the stings of remorse, they abandon themselves to all manner of iniquity, giving loose reins to self-indulgence. Ellicott observes that Paul dwells on the moral loss with intensity: the Greek apelgekotes means literally having lost the capacity for pain — the moral pain that is the natural and healthful consequence of sin. Clarke notes that the verb signifies, first, throwing off all sense of shame and being utterly devoid of pain for committing unrighteous acts; second, being desperate, without hope or desire of reformation; in a word, without remorse and utterly regardless of character or final blessedness. Barnes describes the total want of all emotion on moral subjects as an accurate description of the state of a hardened sinner: he often gives an intellectual assent to truth but without emotion of any kind, the heart insensible as the hard rock. Wesley adds that pain urges the sick to seek a remedy; where there is no pain, remedy is not sought.

voices.html: The account which had been given of natural depravity is followed by a description of the worst of all evils, brought upon men by their own sinful conduct. Having destroyed the sensibilities of the heart and allayed the stings of remorse, they abandon themselves to all manner of iniquity and give loose reins to self-indulgence.

They have lost the capacity of pain, the moral pain which is the natural and healthful consequence of sin against our true natures. Consequently, losing in this their true humanity, they give themselves over to lasciviousness, to work all uncleanness with greediness, trading in all uncleanness as a business.

Being past feeling: wholly hardened in sin, a total want of all emotion on moral subjects. An accurate description of the state of a sinner: he often gives an intellectual assent to the truth, but it is without emotion of any kind; the heart is insensible as the hard rock. They have given themselves over voluntarily.

20.synthesis: But you did not so learn Christ. Calvin draws the contrast sharply: the Gentiles walk in darkness and cannot distinguish right from wrong, but those on whom the truth of God shines ought to live differently. To learn Christ is a phrase used nowhere else in Paul, but Ellicott notes it is easily interpreted by the commoner phrase to know Christ, and that the emphasis on the Christ here distinguishes the Messiah from the merely human teacher, in opposition to what the heathen had learned from their philosophers. Barnes is precise: you have been taught a different thing by Christ; you have been taught that his religion requires you to abandon such a course of life. Wesley reduces it to a single sentence: ye have received the doctrines of Christianity and are taught differently; ye have received the Spirit of Christ and are therefore saved from such dispositions. Robertson notes the sharp contrast with pagan life using outos (so): ye did not so learn the Christ.

voices.html: He now draws a contrast of a Christian life, making it evident how utterly inconsistent it is with the character of a godly man to defile himself with the abominations of the Gentiles. Because the Gentiles walk in darkness they do not distinguish between right and wrong; but those on whom the truth of God shines ought to live in a different manner.

To learn Christ is a phrase not used elsewhere; but easily interpreted by the commoner phrase to know Christ. The name the Christ is here used emphatically in distinction from the merely human teacher, in opposition to the heathen philosophical teachers from whom the pagan world had learned its way of life.

You have been taught a different thing by Christ; you have been taught that his religion requires you to abandon such a course of life. The religion of Christ demands a total change from the manner of living which Paul has been describing in the preceding verses.

21.synthesis: The appeal turns to what they have heard and been taught, with the truth as it is in Jesus as the governing standard. Calvin notes the reproof implicit in this appeal: it corrects that superficial knowledge of the gospel by which many are elated while wholly unacquainted with newness of life, thinking themselves wise while having no actual transformation. Ellicott observes that the rhetorical if so be that is the same particle used in Ephesians 3:2 and Colossians 1:23, indicating no real doubt but strong affirmation: he has heard; he has been taught in him; he knows the truth that is in Jesus. Barnes notes the possible delicate doubt implied as to whether they had attentively listened to his instructions, though Doddridge renders it as seeing ye have heard him. Robertson points to the Gnostic context: Paul identifies the Christ of verse 20 with Jesus of this verse, flatly affirming that there is truth in Jesus in direct opposition to the Cerinthian distinction between the man Jesus and the aeon Christ.

voices.html: To excite their earnestness the more, he not only tells them that they had heard Christ, but employs a still stronger expression, as if to say that this doctrine had not been slightly pointed out but faithfully delivered and explained. This contains a reproof of that superficial knowledge of the gospel by which many are elated, wholly unacquainted with newness of life.

If so be that indicates no real doubt but only that rhetorical doubt which is strong affirmation. Ye heard him and were taught in him: Paul begins with the first means of knowledge, the hearing his voice directly or through his ministers, and proceeds to describe the fuller immersion in Christ as the sphere and atmosphere of all teaching.

If indeed: condition of first class with aorist indicatives, assumed to be true. Paul here identifies Christ of verse 20 and Jesus of this verse, flatly affirming that there is truth in Jesus in direct opposition to the Cerinthian Gnostics who distinguished between the man Jesus and the aeon Christ.

22.synthesis: The content of the truth as it is in Jesus is the putting off of the old man, which is corrupt according to the deceitful lusts. Calvin explains that Paul demands from the Christian man repentance, or a new life, which consists of self-denial and the regeneration of the Holy Spirit. The old man means the natural disposition of man, what we were by nature before being renewed by the Spirit. Ellicott clarifies that the old man is to be put off in relation to the corruption of the true humanity, not in relation to the true humanity itself, and that the phrase which is being marred is a present participle indicating ongoing corruption getting progressively worse. Barnes notes the striking feature: it is possible there had been teachers among them who had not enforced the duties of practical religion as they should have, which explains Paul's emphasis here. Wesley is succinct: the old man is the whole body of sin; all sinful desires are deceitful, promising the happiness they cannot give.

voices.html: He demands from a Christian man repentance, or a new life, which he makes to consist of self-denial and the regeneration of the Holy Spirit. The old man, as we have repeatedly stated in expounding Romans 6, means the natural disposition of man — what we were before being renewed by the Spirit of God.

The old man is to be put off as far as concerns the corruption of the true humanity, not in relation to the true humanity itself. The phrase which is being marred shows an ongoing process of corruption: the old man is not merely old but is getting worse and worse, decaying more and more completely.

That ye lay aside the old man, renounce it: the former mode of life described as the old man. Your whole former life was corrupt and abominable; you lived in the pursuit of pleasure and happiness, sought it in the gratification of the lusts of the flesh, and were entirely in the power of sin and the devil.

23.synthesis: Be renewed in the spirit of your mind. Calvin identifies the spirit of the mind as the mind itself considered in its highest part — not only the inferior appetites or desires manifestly sinful, but that part of the soul most noble and excellent, which must also be renewed. Barnes notes that the Greek word ananeoo means to make new or young again, and the renewal is in the spirit of the mind, pointing to the innermost faculty of the soul as the seat of transformation. Clarke states the comprehensive scope: not merely the general complexion of the mind but its very spirit, all its faculties and powers, must be thoroughly, completely, and universally renewed. Ellicott observes that spirit here is not the Holy Spirit but the human spirit, the mind or inner man considered in its true relation to God; and the process of being made young again contrasts with the decrepitude of the old man, which is growing more corrupt.

voices.html: I understand the spirit of the mind to mean not only the inferior appetites or desires which are manifestly sinful, but also that part of the soul most noble and excellent. And here again Paul brings forward that queen which philosophers are accustomed almost to worship, as if no fault attached to it, and subjects it too to renewal.

The word translated renewed means properly to be made young again, and the process of recovery is described as the natural effect of putting off the decrepitude of the old man. The spirit of the mind is the spiritual nature of the inner man; the human spirit, the mind considered in its true relation to God.

And be renewed in the spirit of your mind: the mind is to be renovated, and not only its general complexion but the very spirit of it, all its faculties and powers must be thoroughly, completely, and universally renewed. Not only the outward conduct but the inward principle from which all conduct flows must be transformed.

24.synthesis: And put on the new man, created according to God in true righteousness and holiness. Calvin states the essential point: the metaphor of putting on the new man indicates that this is an act received, not achieved; the new man is God's creation, not man's manufacture. Adam was at first created after the image of God, reflecting divine righteousness, but that image has been corrupted and can only be restored by grace in Christ. Ellicott extends the thought: to put on the new man is to put on the Lord Jesus Christ, as the beginning in Galatians 3:27, the continuation in Romans 13:14, and the completion in 1 John 3:2. Barnes explains the change so completely that there is no impropriety in speaking of the one who has experienced it as a new man with new feelings, principles, and desires. Wesley makes it simple: the new man is universal holiness, created after the very image of God. Robertson identifies ton kainon anthropon as the brand-new man and notes that kata theon — after the pattern God — is the goal: the new birth aimed at conformity to God, ultimately like God in the end.

voices.html: Adam was at first created after the image of God and reflected, as in a mirror, divine righteousness; but that image has been effaced and is now corrupted. What is here said about the new creation refers to the second creation effected by the grace of Christ: we must receive it, not manufacture it.

To put on the new man is to put on the Lord Jesus Christ, by that divine process of which we have the beginning in Galatians 3:27, the continuation in Romans 13:14, and the completion in 1 John 3:2. In the new man is implied not merely youthfulness but the freshness of a higher nature.

The new man refers to the renovated nature; called in other places the new creature or the new creation, 2 Corinthians 5:17. The change is so great that there is no impropriety in speaking of one who has experienced it as a new man. He has new feelings, principles, and desires. He has laid aside his old principles and practices and commenced a new life.

25.synthesis: From the principle of the new man, all practical exhortations flow like streams from a fountain. The first specific command is to put away lying and speak truth, each one with his neighbor. Calvin notes that from the righteousness of the new man all godly exhortations derive their root and rationale; without this principle, moral rules are of little value, since philosophy can describe virtues but only the new creation can produce them. Ellicott offers a specific reason for truth as the first command: truth is the first condition of mutual confidence, which is the basis of all unity; and since we are members of one another in the body of Christ, deception is self-wounding. Barnes reminds readers that lying was the universal vice of the heathen world: among ancient pagans it was almost universally practised, and the Christian converts had been long addicted to it. Wesley adds the theological grounding: since they are created anew, they are to walk accordingly; deceit is quite repugnant to the intimate union of membership in one another.

voices.html: From this head of doctrine, that is, from the righteousness of the new man, all godly exhortations flow like streams from a fountain. Philosophers take a different method, but in the doctrine of godliness there is no other way than this for regulating the life. He comes now to lay down particular exhortations.

Truth is the first condition of mutual confidence which is the basis of all unity. Hence it is the first duty of that membership one of another which follows from our being one body in Christ. No doubt it is also the first duty to our own humanity and to the God who hateth a lie; but here the appeal is to the body and its unity.

Lying is the universal vice of the heathen world. Among the ancient heathen, as among the moderns, it was almost universally practised. The Christian converts at Ephesus had been long addicted to it; hence the necessity of special caution on that head. The new man, created in true righteousness and holiness, cannot indulge in a vice so directly opposed to its nature.

26.synthesis: Be angry and sin not; let not the sun go down on your wrath. Calvin notes the quotation from Psalm 4:4 and the ambiguity of the Hebrew, which can mean either tremble or be agitated by anger. The key question is whether this is a command to be angry or a permission, and whether anger itself is sinful. Ellicott is clear: anger is not sin, since our Lord himself felt it at the hardness of men's hearts, and it performs a legitimate function as the resentment of righteous indignation. Barnes agrees that anger may exist without sin, but there is special danger in all cases where anger exists that it will be accompanied with sin. Robertson calls this a permissive imperative, not a command to be angry, with the prohibition against sinning as the real point; the settled mood of anger in paroorgismo is the danger. Wesley and Clarke both make the pastoral point: if you are angry at the person as well as the fault, you sin; reprove your brother and be reconciled immediately — lose not one day.

voices.html: Anger itself is not sin, for our Lord himself felt it at the hardness of men's hearts, and it is again and again attributed to God himself in language of human accommodation. In the form of resentment, and above all of righteous indignation, it performs an important function in maintaining moral order.

It is implied here that there may be anger without sin, and that there is special danger, in all cases where there is anger, that it will be accompanied with sin. Anger is a passion too common to require definition and too dangerous to be indulged without the most careful guard lest it proceed too far.

Be ye angry and sin not: permissive imperative, not a command to be angry. Prohibition against sinning as the peril in anger. Quotation from Psalm 4:4. Let not the sun go down upon your wrath: danger in settled mood of anger. Paroorgismos, provocation, from paroorgizo, to exasperate to anger, occurs only in LXX and here in the New Testament.

26.key_tension: Commentators agree anger is not inherently sinful (citing Christ's anger), but divide on whether the imperative is a command (be rightly angry) or a permission with immediate restriction (if angry, do not sin).

27.synthesis: Neither give place to the devil. Calvin has no doubt that Paul's intention is to guard believers against allowing Satan to take possession of their minds, since Scripture uniformly uses this word for the adversary. Barnes applies this specifically to the exhortation of the previous verse: do not yield to the suggestions and temptations of Satan, who would take every opportunity to persuade you to cherish unkind and angry feelings. The heart is deceitful, and seldom more so than when we suppose we are merely defending our rights; many feelings about claiming what is ours are actually produced by the temptations of the devil. Clarke allows an alternative reading: since the word diabolos is sometimes used for a calumniator or tale-bearer, the command may mean do not open your ear to the tale-bearer who whispers accusations and inflames anger. Ellicott notes that the name devil appears in Paul only in his later epistles, while the earlier ones use Satan. Robertson offers the practical rule: present active imperative in prohibition — either stop doing it or do not have the habit.

voices.html: I have no doubt Paul's intention was to guard us against allowing Satan to take possession of our minds and, by keeping in his hands this citadel, to do whatever he pleases. We feel every day how impossible, or at least how difficult, it is to be angry and not to sin; and the difficulty arises from this, that Satan holds his seat in our anger.

This has respect probably to the exhortation in the former verse. Do not yield to the suggestions and temptations of Satan, who would take every opportunity to persuade you to cherish unkind and angry feelings. Many feelings, when we suppose we are merely defending our rights, are produced by the temptations of the devil.

Your adversary will strive to influence your mind and irritate your spirit; watch and pray that he may not get any place in you or ascendancy over you. As diabolos is sometimes used to signify a calumniator or backbiter, it may here mean: do not open your ear to the tale-bearer who uses your anger for his own wicked purposes.

28.synthesis: Let him who stole steal no more; rather let him labor, working with his own hands what is good, so that he may have to give to him who has need. Calvin notes that the prohibition extends beyond the grosser thefts punished by human laws to the more concealed forms of depredation by which we seize what belongs to others; and not merely does Paul forbid unjust taking, he enjoins active assistance to brethren. Barnes explains the social context: theft was almost a universal vice among the heathen, and the Christian converts at Ephesus had been long addicted to it, making special caution necessary. Clarke notes that the apostle here teaches a doctrine different from rabbinic permissiveness toward stealing if a portion was given to the poor; under the Christian system nothing contrary to truth and righteousness could be tolerated. Wesley focuses on the positive command: let him labour so that idleness does not lead him to steal again, and let him earn in order to give, becoming a blessing rather than a burden to his neighbors. Robertson is crisp: clearly here, cease stealing, and even unemployment is no excuse for stealing.

voices.html: This includes not merely the grosser thefts punished by human laws but those of a more concealed nature. He does not simply forbid taking property in an unjust manner; he enjoins active assistance to brethren. Thou who formerly stole must now not only obtain thy subsistence honestly, but must thine own necessities being supplied, have enough to assist others.

Theft was almost a universal vice among the heathen. The Christian converts at Ephesus had been long addicted to it; hence the necessity of special caution on that head. Christianity requires not only that we do not steal but that we engage in honest industry, and that we give to those who are in need from the fruits of our own labor.

Steal no more: clearly here, cease stealing, present active imperative with meketi. The good thing as opposed to his stealing, with his hands that did the stealing. To share with one: present active infinitive of metadidoomi. Even unemployment is no excuse for stealing; labor so as to be able to give.

29.synthesis: Let no corrupt communication proceed from your mouth, but only what is good for building up, as befits the need, that it may give grace to those who hear. Calvin first forbids all language used for inflaming lust, then enjoins speech framed for edification. Barnes explains the word sapros — corrupt, rotten — as applied to putrid vegetables or animal substances, used metaphorically to denote depraved, evil, contaminating speech. Clarke provides extensive lexical support, noting that logos sapros means useless, putrid, unsavory, and obscene words, and that calumnious or reproachful speech of every kind falls under this prohibition. Wesley states the practical principle: only speech that is profitable to speaker and hearers, forwarding them in repentance, faith, or holiness, so that it becomes a means of conveying more grace into their hearts; hence all speech that is not profitable, not edifying, and not apt to minister grace to the hearers is corrupt as if stinking in God's nostrils. Robertson notes the contrast: sapros is rotten, putrid, like rotten fruit, here the opposite of agathos, good.

voices.html: He first forbids believers to use any language employed for the purpose of inflaming lust. Not satisfied with the removal of the vice, he enjoins them to frame their discourse for edification. The genitive may no doubt be variously translated, but the meaning is speech that is useful or profitable, that which may contribute to the good of our neighbors.

The word rendered corrupt means bad, decayed, rotten; applied to putrid vegetables or animal substances; then used in a moral sense to denote that which is depraved, evil, contaminating. Of this character is all impure language; all profaneness; all obscenity; all language that tends to inflame passion or that corrupts the mind.

But that which is good, profitable to the speaker and hearers, to the use of edifying, to forward them in repentance, faith, or holiness, that it may minister grace to the hearers, being a means of conveying more grace into their hearts. All speech that is not profitable, not edifying, not apt to minister grace, is corrupt as stinking in God's nostrils.

30.synthesis: And grieve not the Holy Spirit of God, by whom you were sealed for the day of redemption. This verse, as Ellicott and Calvin both note, refers to all the practical commands given above, since the four cardinal sins forbidden — falsehood, wrath-holding, theft, corrupt speech — are all regarded as grieving the Holy Spirit who indwells believers. Calvin draws the implication: as the Holy Spirit dwells in us, every part of soul and body ought to be devoted to him; and if we give ourselves up to anything impure, we drive him from making his abode with us. Ellicott observes that the expression grieving the Holy Spirit implies a personal relation to a Divine Person capable of grief, which is stronger than quenching the Spirit in 1 Thessalonians 5:19 or resisting the Holy Ghost in Acts 7:51. Barnes addresses this to Christians specifically, proving that it is possible for believers to grieve the Spirit; the word means to afflict with sorrow, to make sad or sorrowful. Robertson makes the tense precise: cease grieving, or do not have the habit of grieving; and adds the personal note, who of us has not sometimes grieved the Holy Spirit?

voices.html: As the Holy Spirit dwells in us, to him every part of our soul and body ought to be devoted. But if we give ourselves up to anything that is impure, we may be said to drive him away from making his abode with us; and, to express this still more familiarly, human affections such as joy and grief are ascribed to the Holy Spirit.

This verse refers to all the practical commands given above. In the expression grieve the Holy Spirit, even more than in the cognate expressions of quenching the Spirit or resisting the Holy Ghost, there is implied a personal relation to a Divine Person capable of being grieved by our transgressions as sins against his perfect holiness.

Grieve not the Holy Spirit of God: cease grieving, or do not have the habit of grieving. Who of us has not sometimes grieved the Holy Spirit? In whom ye were sealed: not in which. The day when final redemption is realized is the day of redemption toward which the sealing points.

31.synthesis: Let all bitterness, wrath, anger, clamor, and evil speaking be put away from you, with all malice. Calvin distinguishes the terms with care: between thumos and orge there is little difference except that the former denotes the power and the latter the act of anger; clamor is the noisy disputes and reproaches that accompany it; and malice is the root behind all of them. Ellicott notes that bitterness or sharpness is an acerbity of temper that shows itself in words and in the general tone of behavior, the first symptom of the forbidden temper; it proceeds through wrath and anger to clamor and evil speaking, each stage intensifying the one before. Barnes insists that Christians are to be calm and serious; harsh contentions and strifes, hoarse brawls and tumults, are to be unknown among them. Wesley gives a vivid pastoral rebuke: I am not angry, but it is my way to speak so — well, then unlearn that way; it is the way to hell. Clarke observes the astonishment that any professing the Christian name should indulge bitterness of spirit, and identifies the censorious as the prime offenders.

voices.html: Between wrath and anger there is little difference except that the former denotes the power and the latter the act; but here the only difference is that wrath is a more sudden attack. He again condemns anger, but on this occasion views it in connection with those offenses by which it is usually accompanied: noisy disputes, reproaches, and the malice that is the root of all.

The first symptom of the temper forbidden is bitterness or sharpness, an acerbity of temper that shows itself in the general tone of character and speech. It proceeds through wrath and anger to clamor, the loud, violent speaking of excited contention, and on to evil speaking and malice, each stage intensifying the one before.

Let all bitterness, the height of settled anger, and wrath, lasting displeasure, and anger, the very first risings of disgust at those that injure you, and clamour, or bawling — I am not angry, says one, but it is my way to speak so: then unlearn that way, it is the way to hell — and evil speaking be put away with all malice.

32.synthesis: Be kind to one another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another as God in Christ forgave you. Calvin shows how kindness and tenderness complete the removal of bitterness: with kindness he contrasts gentleness of countenance, language, and manners; and as this virtue will never reign unless attended by sympathy, he recommends sympathy that not only shares in the distresses of brethren but is affected by everything that happens to them as if we were in their situation. Ellicott provides lexical precision: kindness is gentleness in bearing with wrong, while tenderheartedness is the more positive warmth of sympathy and love, both issuing in the free forgiveness of God in Christ as their model. Barnes finds in Christianity the true secret of politeness: not roughness, crabbedness, or sourness, but benevolence, the desire to make others happy. Wesley reduces the whole to its theological ground: as God, showing himself kind and tenderhearted in the highest degree, hath forgiven you. Robertson draws on Colossians 3:12 for the parallel: keep on becoming kind toward one another, present middle imperative conveying ongoing habitual practice.

voices.html: With bitterness he contrasts gentleness of countenance, language, and manners. As this virtue will never reign unless attended by sympathy, he recommends that we be tenderhearted: not only to sympathize with the distresses of brethren as if they were our own, but to cultivate that true humanity which is affected by everything that happens to them as if we were in their situation.

Kindness is gentleness in bearing with wrong; tenderheartedness is the more positive warmth of sympathy and love. Both issue in free forgiveness, after the model of the universal and unfailing forgiveness of God in Christ to us — the only model we dare to follow, suggested by our Saviour himself in the Lord's Prayer.

Christianity produces true courteousness or politeness. It does not make one rough, crabbed, or sour; nor does it dispose its followers to violate the proper rules of social intercourse. The secret of true politeness is benevolence, or a desire to make others happy; and a Christian should be the most polite of men.

1.synthesis: Paul opens by grounding his apostleship not in personal achievement or ecclesiastical appointment but entirely in the will of God. The salutation is addressed to the saints at Ephesus — holy ones set apart in Christ — and to all the faithful in the surrounding region, indicating the letter's circular character. Calvin observes that calling oneself 'an apostle of Jesus Christ' carries more authority than the general ministry of the gospel, signifying a specific divine commission to found churches and deliver authoritative doctrine. Wesley reads the phrase 'by the will of God' as a deliberate self-denial: not by any merit of his own. Ellicott notes that this exact formulation — apostle 'by the will of God' — appears across Paul's letters to Corinth, Colossae, and Timothy, and signals a calm confidence rather than a defensive posture, used precisely where there was no special occasion for asserting his authority against challengers. Clarke provides rich historical context for Ephesus as the metropolis of Ionia, rebuilt by Lysimachus after the ancient city's destruction, home to the great temple of Artemis. Barnes explores at length the question of authorship and addressees, noting that the phrase 'at Ephesus' is absent from some early manuscripts — a textual puzzle that bears on whether this was a genuinely circular letter.

voices.html: He calls himself 'an apostle of Jesus Christ;' for all to whom has been given the ministry of reconciliation are his ambassadors. The word Apostle, indeed, carries something more; for it is not every minister of the gospel who is an apostle, as we shall afterwards see.

By the will of God — Not by any merit of my own. To the saints who are at Ephesus — And in all the adjacent places. For this epistle is not directed to the Ephesians only, but likewise to all the other churches of Asia.

This phrase, used in 1 Corinthians 1:1; 2 Corinthians 1:1; Colossians 1:1; 2 Timothy 1:1, appears to be St. Paul's ordinary designation of the source of his apostolic mission and authority; used whenever there was nothing peculiar in the occasion of the Epistle to require a more formal enunciation.

2.synthesis: The salutation 'Grace be to you, and peace' is Paul's near-universal greeting across his letters, also taken up by Peter, John, and the Apocalypse. The order is deliberate and theologically freighted: grace must precede peace, because peace with God flows from his free favor, not from our prior merit or moral equilibrium. Ellicott notes that this is Paul's 'all but invariable salutation,' and that the epistle now ascends immediately into 'the heavenly places,' setting its characteristic tone of adoration and cosmic elevation from the very opening. The twin gifts of grace and peace — charis and eirēnē, the Greek and Hebrew blessings combined — signal that the gospel addresses both the Gentile world (grace as divine favor freely given) and the Jewish longing for shalom. Clarke points back to the Romans 1:7 discussion of this formula for the full treatment. The greeting is transitional but not merely formal; it enacts what the whole epistle will unfold: the union of formerly divided peoples in one sphere of divine favor.

voices.html: On this, St. Paul's all but invariable salutation in every Epistle (found also in the Epistles of St. Peter, 2 John, and Apocalypse). The Epistle ascends at once into 'the heavenly places,' naturally catching therefrom the tone of adoration and thanksgiving, dwelling on the election of the children of God by His predestinating love.
Grace be to you — See the note on Romans 1:7.
By mere favour. It is not by your own merit; it is not because you have any claim. This is a favourite doctrine with Paul, as it is with all who love the Lord Jesus in sincerity.

3.synthesis: The great doxology that opens Ephesians (vv. 3–14) begins with a deliberate heaping of cognate terms: 'blessed' (eulogētos), 'blessed us' (eulogēsas), 'blessing' (eulogia). Calvin, citing Bloomfield, notes that such accumulation of related sounds was considered a Greek literary beauty by the ancients, not a blemish, and that Paul deploys it here to fire the hearts of his readers with gratitude. God is praised for blessing believers 'with every spiritual blessing in the heavenly places in Christ' — the whole scope of salvation considered as an already-given heavenly reality, not merely a future hope. Clarke specifies these blessings as 'the pure doctrines of the Gospel, and the abundant gifts and graces of the Holy Spirit, justifying, sanctifying, and building us up.' Wesley explains that God's blessing us is the bestowal of spiritual and heavenly gifts; our blessing God is our solemn, grateful acknowledgment of his essential blessedness. Ellicott stresses that the opening phrase sets the epistle's characteristic direction: it immediately points upward to 'the heavenly places,' the sphere that gives the whole letter its distinctive heavenward orientation, anticipating the prayer of vv. 17–23 and the cosmic warfare of ch. 6.

voices.html: The lofty terms in which he extols the grace of God toward the Ephesians are intended to rouse their hearts to gratitude, to set them all on flame, to fill them even to overflowing with this thought. As to the accumulation of cognate terms in eulogētos eulogēsas and eulogia, such was by the ancients rather sought after as a beauty than avoided as a blemish.

In Ephesians 1:3–6 the Epistle ascends at once into 'the heavenly places,' naturally catching therefrom the tone of adoration and thanksgiving. It dwells on the election of the children of God by His predestinating love — an election to holiness, unto sonship, unto glory and praise.

Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who hath blessed us — God's blessing us is his bestowing all spiritual and heavenly blessings upon us. Our blessing God is the paying him our solemn and grateful acknowledgments, both on account of his own essential blessedness, and of the blessings which he bestows upon us.

4.synthesis: 'He chose us in him before the foundation of the world, that we should be holy and blameless before him' — this verse carries the full weight of the epistle's doctrine of election and stands at the center of longstanding theological debate. Calvin argues with characteristic forcefulness that the foundation and first cause of all our blessings is God's eternal election, entirely free since it preceded any human act or merit. Barnes agrees: these blessings were not conferred by chance but by eternal divine intention, their value greatly enhanced by the fact that God designed them 'from all eternity.' Wesley takes a different path, reading the 'us' as 'both Jews and Gentiles, whom he foreknew as believing in Christ' — an echo of 1 Peter 1:2 that grounds election in divine foreknowledge of faith. Clarke moves further still, reading the verb as God's covenantal decree to bring Gentiles into salvation by Christ, noting that Israel had long considered themselves the exclusive elect people and that Paul here claims that honor for all who believe. The purpose clause — 'that we should be holy and blameless' — is the one point of convergence: all voices insist that election is unto holiness, not merely unto privilege, and that it aims at character formation, not just juridical standing.

voices.html: The foundation and first cause, both of our calling and of all the benefits which we receive from God, is here declared to be his eternal election. The very time when the election took place proves it to be free; for what could we have deserved before we existed? And election does not look to works, as Paul elsewhere teaches: 'Not of works, but of him that calleth.'
As he hath chosen us — Both Jews and gentiles, whom he foreknew as believing in Christ, 1Pet 1:2.

Again it should be, He chose us for Himself. The eternal election of God is inseparably connected with the blessing of the Spirit. This passage stands alone in St. Paul's Epistles in its use of this word 'chosen' in connection with God's eternal purpose 'before the foundation of the world.'

According as he hath chosen us in him — As he has decreed from the beginning of the world to bring us Gentiles to the knowledge of this glorious state of salvation by Christ Jesus. The Jews considered themselves an elect or chosen people, and wished to monopolize the whole of the Divine love and beneficence. The apostle here shews that the Gentiles were equally objects of God's eternal purpose of mercy.

4.key_tension: Calvin and Barnes read the election as God's unconditional sovereign choice of individuals before creation; Wesley reads it as God's foreknowledge of those who would believe; Clarke reads it as a covenantal decree to include Gentiles in salvation, without individual predetermination.

5.synthesis: Having declared eternal election, Paul now adds predestination 'unto the adoption of children by Jesus Christ to himself,' according to the good pleasure of his will. The verse extends the previous election-language into the specific sphere of sonship: those chosen are also foreordained to be received as sons in the household of God. Calvin sees three causes of salvation named across vv. 4–6: the efficient cause (divine election), the mediate cause (adoption through Christ), and the motivating spring (God's good pleasure alone). Barnes explains proorizō (predestinate) as literally 'to set bounds before' — setting a predetermined limit — language that he takes to exclude any conditionality. Wesley, however, reads predestination as God's foreordaining that 'all who afterwards believed should enjoy the dignity of being sons of God, and joint-heirs with Christ,' adding that 'according to the good pleasure of his will' means 'his free, fixed, unalterable purpose to confer this blessing on all those who should believe in Christ, and those only.' The practical effect all voices agree on is identical: the adopted child owes everything to grace and nothing to personal merit, and the goal is the praise of God's glorious grace (v. 6) — not the confirmation of human worth.

voices.html: What follows is intended still further to heighten the commendation of divine grace. Three causes of our salvation are here mentioned: free adoption through Christ, the eternal election which preceded it, and God's own good pleasure as the single spring and motive. That we might direct our minds to nothing else but free grace.

Having predestinated us to the adoption of sons — Having foreordained that all who afterwards believed should enjoy the dignity of being sons of God, and joint-heirs with Christ. According to the good pleasure of his will — According to his free, fixed, unalterable purpose to confer this blessing on all those who should believe in Christ, and those only.

The word used (προοριζω) means, properly, to set bounds before; and then to predetermine. There is the essential idea of setting bounds or limits, and of doing this beforehand. It is not that God determined to do it when it was actually done, but that he intended to do it beforehand. No language could express this more directly.

5.key_tension: Calvin and Barnes read predestination as God's unconditional prior appointment of individuals to adoption; Wesley reads it as God's foreordaining all who would believe to receive sonship — the decree is firm but conditioned on faith.

6.synthesis: 'To the praise of the glory of his grace, wherein he hath made us accepted in the beloved' — the first of three doxological refrains punctuating the great eulogy (vv. 6, 12, 14), each directing the reader back to grace as the ultimate motive and end of the whole saving plan. Barnes notes the Hebraism: 'the praise of the glory of his grace' = 'his glorious grace' — the construction heaps honor upon the attribute. The real tendency of the doctrine of election, in minds properly affected, is not to excite opposition to God but to excite thanksgiving and praise; that is precisely the point of the refrain. Ellicott explains 'to the praise of the glory' as an acknowledgment by all creatures that God's essential glory is best manifested in his grace — that he 'declares his almighty power most chiefly in showing mercy and pity,' citing Exodus 33:18–19 where to the request 'Show me thy glory' the answer is 'I will make my goodness to pass before thee.' Wesley underscores the same point: this is God's 'glorious, free love without any desert on our part.' Clarke notes the Hebrew idiom: 'his grace' qualified by 'glorious' is the apostle's way of saying that in the Gospel's gift of Christ, God's grace is displayed with the same brilliance as his justice and holiness were in the giving of the Law.

voices.html: That is, for the acknowledgment by all God's creatures of the gloriousness of His grace; that God's essential glory is best manifested in His grace — that He 'declares His almighty power most chiefly in showing mercy and pity.' So in Exodus 33:18–19, to the request, 'Show me Thy glory,' the answer is, 'I will make my goodness to pass before thee.'

This is a Hebraism, and means the same as 'to his glorious grace.' The real tendency of the doctrine, in minds that are properly affected, is not to excite opposition to God, or to lead to the charge of partiality, tyranny, or severity; it is to excite thankfulness and praise.
To the praise of the glory of his grace — His glorious, free love without any desert on our part.

7.synthesis: 'In whom we have redemption through his blood, the forgiveness of sins, according to the riches of his grace' — the verse moves from the eternal sphere of election to the historical event of atonement. Redemption (apolytrōsis) means liberation through a ransom price; that price is identified immediately and without softening as the blood of Christ. Ellicott notes that this verse is nearly identical in sense and expression to Colossians 1:14, but points out a significant Greek distinction: the word for 'sins' here (paraptōmata) means 'separate acts of transgression' — concrete, particular trespasses — while Colossians uses the more general word for sin in the abstract. Calvin draws attention to the phrase 'according to the riches of his grace,' arguing that the apostle is still illustrating what he calls the 'material cause' of salvation: Christ's death restores us to the Father's favor, and therefore we ought always to direct our minds to the blood as the means by which divine grace is obtained. Clarke identifies the blood as 'the redemption price paid down for our salvation,' understanding Christ's sacrifice in frankly commercial terms that are consistent with the ransom imagery throughout Paul. Wesley connects redemption to present-tense possession: 'we have — from the moment we believe — redemption... from the guilt and power of sin.'

voices.html: This passage is identical in sense and expression with Colossians 1:14, except that the word here used for 'sins' means, properly, 'separate acts of transgression,' while the word there is the more general word for sin in the abstract. In both passages we have united the two expressions under which our Lord Himself describes His atoning work.

The apostle is still illustrating the material cause — the manner in which we are reconciled to God through Christ. By his death he has restored us to favor with the Father; and therefore we ought always to direct our minds to the blood of Christ, as the means by which we obtain divine grace.

By whom we who believe have — From the moment we believe — Redemption — From the guilt and power of sin. Through his blood — Through what he hath done and suffered for us. According to the riches of his grace — According to the abundant overflowings of his free mercy and favour.

8.synthesis: 'Wherein he hath abounded toward us in all wisdom and prudence' — the 'wherein' refers back to grace, and Paul's point is that God's grace does not merely save but overflows into the additional gifts of wisdom and prudence. Ellicott argues that 'abounded' (eperisseusen) should be rendered 'overflow,' since the word carries a stronger emphasis than our English 'abound' has retained — it signifies a richness that not only fills but spills over into further gifts. The grace that brings salvation brings with it illumination. Calvin identifies this clause as introducing what he calls the 'formal cause' of salvation — the preaching of the gospel, by which God's goodness has been abounded toward believers — and notes a grammatical debate among ancient and modern commentators about whether 'wisdom and prudence' qualify God's own action in planning salvation or describe gifts imparted to believers and apostles. Clarke reads the verse as referring specifically to the apostles: they were given 'the most complete instructions in heavenly things by the inspiration of his Spirit; and at the same time prudence, that we might know when and where to preach the Gospel so that it might be effectual.' Barnes sees grace as having been 'liberally manifested' — not stinted or confined — with the wisdom of God evident in the plan that simultaneously honored his law and saved fallen humanity.

voices.html: It should be, which He made to overflow to us in all wisdom and prudence — the word 'overflow' having an emphasis which our word 'abound' has lost, and signifying here that the richness of God's grace not only fills the soul with the blessing of salvation, but overflows into the additional gifts of 'all wisdom and prudence.'

He now comes to the formal cause, the preaching of the gospel, by which the goodness of God 'hath abounded toward us.' The preaching of the gospel is the formal cause of our salvation, and is therefore here mentioned as one of the principal gifts of divine grace.

Wherein he hath abounded — That is, in the dispensation of mercy and goodness by Christ Jesus. In all wisdom and prudence — Giving us apostles the most complete instructions in heavenly things by the inspiration of his Spirit; and at the same time prudence, that we might know when and where to preach the Gospel so that it might be effectual to the salvation of those who heard it.

9.synthesis: 'Having made known unto us the mystery of his will' — the mystery that was once hidden is now disclosed. All the commentators agree that 'mystery' (mystērion) in Paul's usage never means something incomprehensible or impenetrable (as the English word often implies today) but something previously concealed and now revealed. Ellicott traces the term through 1 Corinthians 2:7, Matthew 13:11, and Revelation, confirming the consistent usage: 'something which was hid from all ages, but is now made manifest.' Calvin notes that some of Paul's hearers were alarmed at the novelty of his doctrine, and Paul therefore preemptively calls it a 'mystery' yet one which God has now been pleased to reveal — arresting the objection before it can form. Clarke identifies the specific content of the mystery: that the Gentiles should ever be received into the Church of God with all the privileges of Israel, without circumcision or the Mosaic rites, was the great secret 'which had never been published before; and now revealed only to the apostles.' Wesley emphasizes that this mystery was 'but darkly discovered under the law; is now totally hid from unbelievers; and has heights and depths which surpass all the knowledge even of true believers.' Barnes adds that in the New Testament the word always refers to something once concealed but now disclosed, never to something above comprehension.

voices.html: The word 'mystery' properly signifies a thing which was hid from all ages, but is now made manifest. So our Lord evidently uses it (in Matthew 13:11; Mark 4:11; Luke 8:10). It is a regular feature of Paul's vocabulary for the previously hidden counsel of God now disclosed in the gospel.

Having made known unto us the mystery — That the Gentiles should ever be received into the Church of God, and have all the privileges of the Jews, without being obliged to submit to circumcision, and perform the rites and ceremonies of the Jewish law was a mystery — a hidden thing which had never been published before; and now revealed only to the apostles.

Having made known to us — By his word and by his Spirit — The mystery of his will — The gracious scheme of salvation by faith, which depends on his own sovereign will alone. This was but darkly discovered under the law; is now totally hid from unbelievers; and has heights and depths which surpass all the knowledge even of true believers.

10.synthesis: 'That in the dispensation of the fullness of times he might gather together in one all things in Christ' — this verse gives the cosmic scope and telos of the mystery: the anakephalaiōsis, the 'gathering to a head' of all things in Christ at the appointed fullness of time. Ellicott argues that the connection in the English version is erroneous and that the verse should read: the mystery was purposed 'for administration of the fullness of the appointed seasons, to gather together' all things — making the verse about God's stewardship of history's appointed moments, not merely a chronological statement. Calvin, citing Galatians 4:4, calls the appointed period the 'fit and proper season' — 'let human presumption restrain itself, and, in judging of the succession of events, bow to the providence of God.' Barnes identifies oikonomia with stewardship and administration — 'a plan for the management of any sort of business' — and notes that the gathering of all things in Christ looks forward to the eschatological completion of God's purpose. Wesley reads it as the re-union of all creation: 'He might gather together into one in Christ — might recapitulate, re-unite, and place in order again under Christ, their common Head — all things which are in heaven, and on earth.' Clarke supplies the Greek economy of the term and notes that 'all the dispersed families of mankind' are gathered under this one administration of grace.

voices.html: The words should be connected with the previous verse, and translated: which He purposed in Himself for administration of the fullness of the appointed seasons, to gather together, &c. The word 'dispensation' is usually applied to the action of servants of God; here it is applied to God's own stewardship of the whole of time.

That no man may inquire why one time rather than another was selected, the apostle anticipates such curiosity, by calling the appointed period the fit and proper season, as he also did in a former epistle (Galatians 4:4). Let human presumption restrain itself, and in judging of the succession of events, let it bow to the providence of God.

That in the dispensation of the fullness of the times — In this last administration of God's fullest grace, which took place when the time appointed was fully come. He might gather together into one in Christ — Might recapitulate, re-unite, and place in order again under Christ, their common Head. All things which are in heaven, and on earth — All angels and men, whether living or dead, in the Lord.

11.synthesis: 'In whom also we have obtained an inheritance, being predestinated according to the purpose of him who worketh all things after the counsel of his own will' — here the 'we' narrows from the general elect to the Jewish believers specifically, as distinct from the 'ye' (Gentiles) of v. 13. Calvin notes the shift: Paul begins to take notice of separate classes after speaking generally of all the elect, associating himself and other Jewish believers as 'the first fruits of Christianity.' Clarke identifies the inheritance as the fulfillment of what was promised to Abraham and his spiritual seed — the adoption of sons, the kingdom of heaven — signified in type by Israel's possession of the promised land, but now translated into its spiritual reality. Wesley specifies 'the glorious inheritance of the heavenly Canaan,' adding a pointed note on the phrase 'who worketh all things after the counsel of his own will': this is 'not an arbitrary will, but flowing from the rectitude of his nature; else, what security would there be that it would be his will to keep his word even with the elect?' Barnes notes that the reference is primarily to Jewish believers but may include all Christians, and that 'obtained an inheritance' confirms they were partakers in the promise made to Israel's spiritual descendants.

voices.html: Hitherto he has spoken generally of all the elect; he now begins to take notice of separate classes. When he says 'we,' he speaks of himself and of the Jews, or, perhaps more correctly, of all who were the first fruits of Christianity; and afterwards he comes to the Ephesians. It tended not a little to confirm the faith of the Ephesian converts, that he associated them with himself and the other believers, who might be said to be the first-born in the faith.

In whom — Christ Jesus; also we — believing Jews have obtained an inheritance — what was promised to Abraham and his spiritual seed, viz. the adoption of sons, and the kingdom of heaven, signified by the privileges under the Mosaic dispensation, and the possession of the promised land.

Through whom we — Jews. Also have obtained an inheritance — The glorious inheritance of the heavenly Canaan, to which, when believers, we were predestinated according to the purpose of him that worketh all things after the counsel of his own will — The unalterable decree, 'He that believeth shall be delivered;' which will is not an arbitrary will, but flowing from the rectitude of his nature.

12.synthesis: 'That we should be to the praise of his glory, who first trusted in Christ' — Jewish believers are identified as those who hoped in Christ beforehand (pro-ēlpikotes), before the full revelation came to the Gentiles. Ellicott notes that the word translated 'first trusted' (or 'first hoped') is better rendered 'who have hoped beforehand' — that is, who hoped in the Messiah prior to the Gentiles' inclusion, not merely who were the first sequential believers. Calvin frames the point theologically: 'we must eventually become illustrations of the glory of God, if we are nothing but vessels of his mercy.' The word 'glory' here, he says, denotes by way of eminence that which shines most peculiarly in God — his goodness. Wesley offers a striking expansion of what election to glory means practically: 'he that believes is not only elected to salvation (if he endures to the end), but is fore-appointed of God to walk in holiness, to the praise of his glory.' This is Wesley's 'gospel predestination' — chosen not for passive safety but for active Christlikeness. Clarke identifies the 'we' as Paul and his fellow Jewish apostles, who as 'the first fruits' were the first means of spreading the gospel throughout the world.

voices.html: That the reference here is to the first Christians, in contradistinction to the Gentiles of the next verse, is clear. The meaning of 'who first hoped' (or, more properly, who have hoped beforehand) refers to those who hoped in the Messiah prior to the full revelation coming to the Gentiles.

We must eventually become illustrations of the glory of God, if we are nothing but vessels of his mercy. The word by way of eminence denotes, in a peculiar manner, that which shines in the goodness of God; for there is nothing that is more peculiarly his own, or in which he desires more to be glorified, than goodness.

That we — Jews. Who first believed — Before the gentiles. So did some of them in every place. Here is another branch of the true gospel predestination: he that believes is not only elected to salvation (if he endures to the end), but is fore-appointed of God to walk in holiness, to the praise of his glory.

13.synthesis: 'In whom ye also trusted, after that ye heard the word of truth, the gospel of your salvation: in whom also after that ye believed, ye were sealed with that Holy Spirit of promise' — the Gentiles' inclusion is the third movement of the eulogy. Barnes identifies the two gospel epithets as carefully chosen: 'the word of truth' (a message unmixed with Jewish tradition or Gentile philosophy) and 'the gospel of your salvation' (the good news that brings salvation to you). Sealing with the Spirit follows faith — Ellicott notes the grammatical irregularity here but reads the whole clause as governed by 'ye were sealed.' Wesley gives the richest account of sealing: it implies 'a full impression of the image of God on their souls' and 'a full assurance of receiving all the promises, whether relating to time or eternity.' Calvin notes that Paul's object is to show that both Jewish and Gentile believers had the same faith and therefore the same standing. Clarke stresses that the Spirit received upon believing is 'the earnest, foretaste, and pledge of the heavenly inheritance,' a theme picked up in the very next verse. The sealing is not a second or higher experience but the proper consequence of the hearing and believing that precede it.

voices.html: In whom ye — Gentiles — likewise believed, after ye had heard the gospel. Ye were sealed by that Holy Spirit of promise — Holy both in his nature and in his operations, and promised to all the children of God. The sealing seems to imply a full impression of the image of God on their souls; a full assurance of receiving all the promises, whether relating to time or eternity.

Heard the word of truth — The gospel; called the word or message of truth, the word of God. The phrase 'the word of truth' means 'the true word or message.' It was a message unmixed with Jewish traditions or Gentile philosophy. The gospel of your salvation — The gospel bringing salvation to you.

He associates the Ephesians with himself, and with the rest of those who were the first fruits; for he says that they, in like manner, trusted in Christ. His object is, to shew that both had the same faith; and therefore we must supply the word from the twelfth verse.

14.synthesis: 'Which is the earnest of our inheritance until the redemption of the purchased possession, unto the praise of his glory' — the Holy Spirit as arrabōn (earnest, down payment) is a rich metaphor drawn from commercial and legal practice. Calvin explains: the arrabōn is 'the first part of the price paid in any contract, as an earnest and security of the remainder, and which therefore is not taken back, but kept till the residue is paid.' This distinguishes it from a pledge (which is returned when the debt is paid) — the earnest remains and guarantees completion. Barnes reads the verse practically: the Spirit will be imparted to believers 'until we enter on that inheritance' — it is a continuous present possession securing a future completion. Wesley connects the two realities: the Spirit is 'both a pledge and a foretaste of our inheritance,' and this sealing will continue 'till the church, which he has purchased with his own blood, shall be fully delivered from all sin and sorrow, and advanced to everlasting glory.' Clarke identifies the 'redemption of the purchased possession' as the full liberation of body and soul at the resurrection, noting that the same Spirit who seals us now will raise us then. The refrain 'to the praise of his glory' closes the great eulogy of vv. 3–14 on the same doxological note with which it began.

voices.html: The original word arrabōn seems properly to denote the first part of the price that is paid in any contract, as an earnest and security of the remainder, and which therefore is not taken back, but kept till the residue is paid to complete the whole sum. And thus it differs from a pledge, which is given for security but redeemed and restored when the contract is completed.

Who, thus sealing us, is an earnest — Both a pledge and a foretaste of our inheritance. Till the redemption of the purchased possession — Till the church, which he has purchased with his own blood, shall be fully delivered from all sin and sorrow, and advanced to everlasting glory. To the praise of his glory — Of his glorious wisdom, power, and mercy.

The word 'inheritance' has a correspondent meaning. It is a present possession which shall be developed into a more precious future. 'We are very members, incorporate in the mystical body of Christ, and also heirs through hope of His everlasting kingdom.' Until the redemption — the meaning is, we have the Holy Spirit as the pledge that that shall be ours, and the Holy Spirit will be imparted to us until we enter on that inheritance.

15.synthesis: 'Wherefore I also, after I heard of your faith in the Lord Jesus and love unto all the saints, cease not to give thanks for you' — Paul moves from the great doxology to his personal prayer. The formula 'after I heard of your faith' has puzzled commentators, since Paul spent nearly three years in Ephesus and knew the church intimately; this sounds like language one uses for a congregation one has never met. Ellicott notes that this has been pressed by critics as evidence the letter was not primarily addressed to Ephesus, pointing out a near-exact parallel in Colossians 1:4 (a church Paul had not personally visited). Barnes responds directly: this inference is not conclusive — Paul had been away from Ephesus for some years when he wrote from prison, and it is natural language for updated report of their continuing faith. Calvin suggests the 'faith' Paul has heard of is not their initial conversion but their ongoing steadfastness: 'the opinion which he had formed respecting them was highly favorable.' Clarke reads faith and love as complementary — 'cordial reception of the Christian religion, amply proved by their love to all the Christians,' noting that 'love' likely means not only the affection but 'all the fruits of love — benevolence, and kind offices of every description.'

voices.html: These words have an almost exact parallel in Colossians 1:4, addressed there to a church which St. Paul had not seen, and have been quoted in support of the belief that this Epistle cannot have been addressed, properly and solely, to the well-known Ephesian Church. They are not, however, decisive, for we have a similar expression to Philemon, St. Paul's own convert.

This is one of the passages usually relied on by those who suppose that this epistle was not written to the Ephesians. But this inference is not conclusive. Paul had been some years absent from Ephesus when this epistle was written, and during that time he would hear of them only as he might hear of any other church at a distance.

This thanksgiving was not simply an expression of his ardent love to the Ephesians. He congratulated them before God, that the opinion which he had formed respecting them was highly favorable. Observe here, that under faith in the Lord Jesus, Paul includes generally the whole excellence of Christian character.

16.synthesis: 'Cease not to give thanks for you, making mention of you in my prayers' — Paul's persistent intercession is the bridge between the doxology and the specific petitions that follow (vv. 17–19). Ellicott observes that this combination of thanksgiving and prayer is characteristic of Paul: it appears in Romans 1:8–9, Philippians 1:3–4, Colossians 1:3–4, 1 Thessalonians 1:2–3, 2 Timothy 1:3, Philemon 1:4 — and that the notable omission of thanksgiving in Galatians, and prayer alone in Corinthians, reveals how deliberate the pattern is. Calvin adds that to thanksgiving Paul joins prayer 'in order to excite them to additional progress,' noting that spiritual benefits already received can become a kind of complacency if not paired with prayer for further growth: 'nothing is more dangerous than to be satisfied with that measure of spiritual benefits which has been already received.' Wesley keeps it simple: 'I cease not — in all my solemn addresses to God — to give thanks for you.' Barnes focuses on what this passage reveals about Paul's pastoral love: 'far distant from them, and expected to see them no more,' he had faith in prayer as the means of their continued advance in knowledge and grace. The intercession is not occasional but perpetual, not general but specific — 'making mention of you.'

voices.html: Almost all St. Paul's Epistles are introduced by this union of thanksgiving and prayer, which is, indeed, characteristic of the right harmony of all Christian worship. In the Galatian Epistle the omission of both is characteristic; in the two Epistles to the Corinthians thanksgiving alone is expressed. The union of both marks a church in health.

To thanksgiving, as his custom is, he adds prayer, in order to excite them to additional progress. Nothing is more dangerous than to be satisfied with that measure of spiritual benefits which has been already received. In spiritual affairs, to stand still is to go backward.

Paul was far distant from them, and expected to see them no more. But he had faith in prayer, and he sought that they might advance in knowledge and in grace. What was the particular subject of his prayers he mentions in the following verses.

17.synthesis: 'That the God of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of glory, may give unto you the spirit of wisdom and revelation in the knowledge of him' — the first petition: deeper knowledge of God through the Spirit. The title 'God of our Lord Jesus Christ' acknowledges Christ's own human and mediatorial relationship to the Father — Ellicott cites John 20:17 ('I ascend unto my God and your God') and notes that Christ distinguished his peculiar relation to the Father from ours even after the resurrection. 'Father of glory' is a Hebrew construction meaning 'glorious Father,' the Father who is the source and possessor of all glory, and from whom the 'glorious inheritance' of v. 18 will come. Barnes reads the title as expressing the same idea: God is here addressed as the one who 'appointed Jesus as the Mediator,' and praying in that name grounds the petition. The gift sought is 'a spirit of wisdom and revelation' — not a new revelation distinct from the gospel, as both Calvin and Wesley are clear, but a deepening of the Spirit's illuminating work on the truth already given. Calvin: 'they needed increase, that, being endowed with a larger measure of the Spirit, and being more and more enlightened, they might more clearly and fully hold their present views.' Wesley: this is the Spirit 'common to all real Christians' in its deepening operation, not a special apostolic gift.

voices.html: See John 20:17, 'I ascend unto My Father and your Father; and to My God and your God.' After His resurrection, our Lord distinguished emphatically between His peculiar relation to God the Father and that relation in which we His members call God 'our Father.' St. Paul's usual phrase is 'God our Father,' or 'God the Father'; this is chosen with deliberate reference to the Son's own words.

Did they not possess these? Yes; but at the same time they needed increase, that, being endowed with a larger measure of the Spirit, and being more and more enlightened, they might more clearly and fully hold their present views. The knowledge of the godly is never so pure, but that some dimness or obscurity hangs over their spiritual vision.

That the Father of that infinite glory which shines in the face of Christ may give you the Spirit of wisdom and revelation — The same who is the Spirit of promise is also, in the progress of the faithful, the Spirit of wisdom and revelation; making them wise unto salvation, and revealing to them the deep things of God. He is here speaking of that wisdom and revelation which are common to all real Christians.

18.synthesis: 'The eyes of your understanding being enlightened' — Ellicott flags a significant textual note: the true reading is 'the eyes of your heart' (kardias), not 'understanding,' and the substitution of 'understanding' in later manuscripts and versions reflects a drive for simplicity that loses the original's depth. The heart in biblical usage signifies the inner person in its entirety — thinking, willing, feeling — and locating spiritual perception there means the whole self must be opened, not merely the rational faculty. Calvin agrees: 'the Hebrews frequently employ it to denote the rational powers of the soul, though more strictly, being the seat of the affections, it means the will or desire.' Clarke elaborates: 'as the eye is not light in itself and can neither see nor enlighten itself but must receive light from an external source, so the understanding is to the soul.' Paul then states three things he prays they may know: (1) the hope of God's calling — what they are called to hope for; (2) the riches of the glory of God's inheritance in the saints — the treasure prepared for them; (3) the exceeding greatness of his power toward believers (v. 19). Wesley: 'ye may experimentally and delightfully know what are the blessings which God has called you to hope for by his word and his Spirit.' Barnes notes the rich imagery and the bold claim that divine power is accessible to ordinary believers.

voices.html: The true reading is of your heart, for which the words 'of your understanding' have been substituted, so as to yield a simpler expression. The heart is similarly spoken of in relation to spiritual perception in Romans 1:21; 1 Corinthians 2:9; it signifies the inner man in his entirety; and the phrase here conveys the truth that for the knowledge of God all the faculties of man, not the intellect only, must be enlightened.

A comparison, suggested by its excellence, reminds us how unfit we are to receive this elevated knowledge; for the power of God cannot be comprehended by our natural understanding. The eyes of the heart must be opened and illuminated before we can perceive divine things.

The eyes of your understanding being enlightened — The understanding is that power or faculty in the soul by which knowledge or information is received; and as Philo expresses it: 'What the eye is to the body, the understanding is to the soul;' and as the eye is not light in itself and can neither see nor enlighten itself, but must receive light from an external source, so must the mind receive divine light from the Spirit of God.

19.synthesis: 'And what is the exceeding greatness of his power to us-ward who believe, according to the working of his mighty power' — the third thing Paul prays they may know is an accumulation of four distinct Greek terms for power: dynamis (force or capacity), energeia (working or energy), kratos (might), and ischus (strength). Barnes notes that the apostle is 'labouring under the greatness of his theme, and wants words to express the magnitude of his conception.' Each term adds a dimension: dynamis is capacity (which may be latent); energeia specifies that this power is actually active and working; kratos is prevailing might; ischus is inherent strength. Ellicott translates the phrase as 'the working of the might of His strength' — power not in reserve but deployed to its full measure. Calvin resists limiting the clause to one referent: it is 'not restricted to one class — in the next, it has a general application,' meaning the power is both that which raised Christ (v. 20) and that which works in all who believe. Wesley gives the pastoral application: God's power is 'toward us who believe — both in quickening our dead souls, and preserving them in spiritual life.' The verse sets up the christological illustration of vv. 20–22, where the believer's experience of power is grounded in Christ's own resurrection and exaltation.

voices.html: More correctly (see margin), the working of the might of His strength. The word 'power' is a general word for force, which may be latent. St. Paul, therefore, adds that this power of God is not latent; it actually works 'according to,' that is, up to the full measure of 'the might of the strength' of God — a threefold accumulation for purposes of emphasis.

There is much emphasis and energy of expression here, as if the apostle were labouring under the greatness of his theme, and wanted words to express the magnitude of his conception. He uses four words to denote the divine power — dynamis, energeia, kratos, ischus — the greatest accumulation of power terms in the New Testament.

And what the exceeding greatness of his power toward us who believe — Both in quickening our dead souls, and preserving them in spiritual life. According to the power which he exerted in Christ, raising him from the dead — By the very same almighty power whereby he raised Christ; for no less would suffice.

20.synthesis: 'Which he wrought in Christ, when he raised him from the dead, and set him at his own right hand in the heavenly places' — the resurrection and exaltation of Christ are presented as the supreme demonstration of the divine power described in v. 19. Barnes presses the physical reality of the resurrection vividly: it meant imparting life to a cold and still frame, reopening the arteries and veins, teaching the heart to beat and the lungs to breathe — 'it is impossible to conceive of a more direct exertion of creative power.' Calvin uses this verse to address a pastoral difficulty: the power of God working in believers is presently hidden and unimpressive from the outside; 'In what do we excel the children of the world but in this, that our condition appears more wretched?' The answer is that our life is hidden with Christ (Col. 3:3), and the same power that raised him will be manifest in us at the last day.
