Clarke identifies the seating 'at his own right hand' as Christ being given 'the highest honors and dignities as Mediator between God and man' — Philippians 2:9 — 'the right hand is the place of friendship, honor, confidence, and authority.' Ellicott notes the shift in emphasis from the earlier Pauline letters (where the resurrection's chief stress is its spiritual meaning) to the Epistles of the Captivity, where the exaltation of Christ in his risen humanity becomes central — the Son of Man enthroned.

voices.html: The power which was then exerted was as great as that of creation. It was imparting life to a cold and still frame. It was to open again the arteries and veins, and teach the heart to beat and the lungs to heave. It is impossible to conceive of a more direct exertion of creative power than was evinced in the resurrection of the Lord Jesus.

With the greatest propriety does he enjoin us to contemplate this power in Christ; for in us it is hitherto concealed. 'My strength,' says he, 'is made perfect in weakness' (2 Corinthians 12:9). In what do we excel the children of the world but in this, that our condition appears more wretched? Our life is hid with Christ in God.

Set him at his own right hand in the heavenly places — Gave him, as mediator between God and man, the highest honors and dignities (Philippians 2:9); in which state of exaltation he transacts all the affairs of his Church, and rules the universe. The right hand is the place of friendship, honor, confidence, and authority.

21.synthesis: 'Far above all principality, and power, and might, and dominion, and every name that is named, not only in this world, but also in that which is to come' — a comprehensive enumeration of all conceivable powers, placed beneath the exalted Christ. Ellicott notes that the four terms are 'intended to include all possible forms of power,' corresponding to the exhaustive enumeration of Philippians 2:10 ('things in heaven, and things in earth, and things under the earth'). 'Principality' and 'power' (archē and exousia) are used in Luke 12:11 for earthly rulers and in Ephesians 6:12 for spiritual powers — the terms deliberately span the visible and invisible. Calvin notes that 'from the diversity of names we conclude that there are various orders of angels; but to attempt to settle these with the presumptuous curiosity of the Schools is not lawful.' Wesley ranges further: Christ is 'invested with uncontrollable authority over all demons in hell, all angels in heaven, and all the princes and potentates on earth.' The phrase 'every name that is named' functions as a catch-all: whatever being exists with a title, in whatever era or age, is subject to Christ. Barnes: 'the main idea is, that the Lord Jesus was exalted to the highest conceivable dignity and honour.'

voices.html: The words here used are intended to include all possible forms of power, corresponding to the exhaustive enumeration in Philippians 2:10, 'of things in heaven, and things in earth, and things under the earth.' The words rendered 'principality and power' are used both for earthly powers (Luke 12:11; Titus 3:1) and for spiritual powers (Ephesians 6:12; Colossians 2:15).

All these names, there can be no doubt, are applied to angels, who are so denominated because, by means of them, God exercises his power, might, and dominion. From the diversity of names we conclude that there are various orders of angels; but to attempt to settle these with the presumptuous curiosity of the Schools is not lawful.

Far above all principality, and power, and might, and dominion — That is, God hath invested him with uncontrollable authority over all demons in hell, all angels in heaven, and all the princes and potentates on earth. And every name that is named — We know the king is above all, though we cannot name all the officers of his court. So we know that Christ is above all, though we are not able to name all his subjects.

22.synthesis: 'And hath put all things under his feet, and gave him to be the head over all things to the church' — the subjugation of all things (citing Psalm 8:6) is distinguished from the headship given specifically to the church. The 'all things under his feet' is universal cosmic dominion; the 'head over all to the church' is a special gift of governance and life given for the church's sake. Calvin insists this is not 'a mere honorary title' but genuine command and government of the universe — and polemically targets those who would give this headship to Rome's bishop: 'Since Christ alone is called the head, it is plain that all others are members.' Wesley describes the headship as twofold — 'an head both of guidance and government, and likewise of life and influence, to the whole and every member of it' — so that members have 'a continual and effectual communication of activity, growth, and strength from him, as the natural body from its head.' Barnes catalogues what it means for the universe to be under Christ's control for the church's benefit: all elements, winds and waves, nations, angels, providences are directed toward the welfare of God's people. Ellicott connects this to Psalm 8:6 applied in 1 Corinthians 15:25–28 and notes that the subjugation language implies an ongoing spiritual war that will continue until the appointed end.

voices.html: He was made the head of the Church, on the condition that he should have the administration of all things. The apostle shews that it was not a mere honorary title, but was accompanied by the entire command and government of the universe. The metaphor of a head denotes the highest authority. Since Christ alone is called 'the head,' all others are members.

And he hath given him to be head over all things to the church — An head both of guidance and government, and likewise of life and influence, to the whole and every member of it. All these stand in the nearest union with him, and have as continual and effectual a communication of activity, growth, and strength from him, as the natural body from its head.

And gave him to be the head over all things to the Church — Appointed him to be the supreme Ruler, with reference to the church, or for its benefit and welfare. The universe is under his control and direction for the welfare of his people — all the elements, the winds and waves, the seas and storms, the nations of the earth, all angels and providences are under his direction.

23.synthesis: 'Which is his body, the fulness of him that filleth all in all' — the church as the body of Christ, and as his plērōma (fullness), is the epistle's culminating christological claim. Ellicott devotes careful attention to the word plērōma ('fullness'), noting it is used in a technical and almost specialized sense in the Epistles of the Captivity — with clear reference to speculations about the divine nature and its emanations already anticipating Gnosticism. The word derives from a verb meaning first 'to fill,' then 'to fulfil' or 'to complete.' Calvin offers the most striking reading: 'the Son of God reckons himself in some measure imperfect until he is united to us.' The church is Christ's body not in a metaphorical sense only but in the sense that without his members Christ's fullness is not yet expressed in the world — 'not until we are along with him does he possess all his parts, or wish to be regarded as complete.' Clarke frames it more simply: 'from him, as head, the church receives light, life, and intelligence.' Barnes is careful to distinguish this from any suggestion that God needs the church: plērōma means that which fills up or completes — the church fills out and completes the expression of Christ in the world without implying any deficiency in Christ's divine nature itself. Barnes cites 1 Corinthians 11:3, 12:27, and Ephesians 4:15–16 as parallel passages on the church-as-body metaphor.

voices.html: This is the highest honor of the Church, that, until He is united to us, the Son of God reckons himself in some measure imperfect. What consolation is it for us to learn, that, not until we are along with him, does he possess all his parts, or wish to be regarded as complete! Hence, in the First Epistle to the Corinthians, when Paul discusses the metaphor of a human body, he includes under the single name of Christ the whole Church.

The word pleroma, 'fulness,' is used in a definite and almost technical sense in the Epistles of the Captivity, having clear reference to the speculations as to the Divine Nature and the emanations from it, already anticipating the future Gnosticism. The word is derived from a verb signifying, first, to 'fill;' next, to 'fulfil' or 'to complete.'

The word here rendered fulness — plērōma — means, properly, that which fills up or completes. The church fills out and completes the expression of Christ in the world. Whatever of peace, or hope, or purity believers have has been produced by his agency on the soul — he who filleth all in all is present in and through his body, the church.

1.synthesis: 'And you hath he quickened, who were dead in trespasses and sins' — the word 'quickened' is supplied from v. 5, and the chapter opens in medias res, extending the thought of 1:19–20 (the exceeding greatness of divine power) into its application to the Ephesian converts. Wesley holds the strongest statement: they were 'not only diseased, but dead; absolutely void of all spiritual life; and as incapable of any spiritual action or motion as a dead man is of natural action.' Calvin explains the chapter's structure: it is an ἐπεξεργασία (an exposition with illustration) of the previous statements about grace — 'To bring home more effectually to the Ephesians the general doctrine of divine grace, he reminds them of their former condition.' Barnes reads the chapter in continuity with ch. 1:19: the same power that raised Christ (vv. 20–22) must be the power that raises sinners from spiritual death — the two are not separable illustrations but a single argument about the scope of divine omnipotence. The pairing of 'trespasses' (paraptōmata) and 'sins' (hamartiai) — as Ellicott notes — covers both specific acts of transgression and the general condition of missing the mark, marking the comprehensiveness of the spiritual death described.

voices.html: And he hath quickened you — In the nineteenth and twentieth verses of the preceding chapter, St. Paul spoke of God's working in them by the same almighty power whereby he raised Christ from the dead. He here resumes the thread of his discourse. Who were dead — Not only diseased, but dead; absolutely void of all spiritual life; and as incapable of any spiritual action or motion as a dead man is of natural action.

This is an exposition accompanied by an illustration. To bring home more effectually to the Ephesians the general doctrine of Divine grace, he reminds them of their former condition. This application consists of two parts: 'Ye were formerly lost; but now God, by his grace, has rescued you from destruction.'

The great object is to illustrate the subject which was commenced in the previous chapter — the greatness of the POWER of God, evinced in the salvation of his people. The great manifestation of his power had been in raising up the Lord Jesus from the dead; and Paul here shows that the same power is evinced in the spiritual resurrection of sinners from the death of sin.

2.synthesis: 'Wherein in time past ye walked according to the course of this world, according to the prince of the power of the air, the spirit that now worketh in the children of disobedience' — the verse identifies two external powers that governed the pre-conversion life: the age of this world (its collective momentum of sin) and the demonic ruler who operates through it. Ellicott distinguishes two Greek words both translated 'world': aiōn (the appointed period with its vanity and transitoriness, as in 'be not conformed to this world' of Romans 12:2) and kosmos (its material and sensuous character). The combination describes both the world's temporal spirit and its material seductiveness. Wesley gives the most vivid account of the demonic prince: 'the effect of which power all may perceive, though all do not understand the cause; a power unspeakably penetrating and widely diffused; but yet, as to its baneful influences, beneath the orb of believers — the evil spirits are united under one head, the seat of whose dominion is in the air.' Calvin identifies the 'course of this world' as 'the corrupt principles and morals, and particularly the idolatrous customs, of the heathen world.' Barnes frames the verse as explaining how the spiritual death of v. 1 manifested in practice: they 'walked' in it — life was a continuous course of sin, not occasional lapses — driven by the world's momentum and satanic influence combined.

voices.html: According to the prince of the power of the air — The effect of which power all may perceive, though all do not understand the cause of it: a power unspeakably penetrating and widely diffused; but yet, as to its baneful influences, beneath the orb of believers. The evil spirits are united under one head, the seat of whose dominion is in the air.

Here again are united the two words often rendered by 'world,' the former signifying simply 'the age,' or appointed period of this visible universe, the latter its material and sensuous character. When we are warned against the one (as in Romans 12:2, 'Be not conformed to this world') it is against the vanity — that is, the transitoriness and unreality — of earthly things; when warned against the other, against their seductive materialism.

According to the course of this world — In conformity with the customs and manners of the world at large. The word here rendered 'course' denotes particularly the corrupt and wicked age of which Satan is the prince — the spirit and maxims of the ungodly world, under the influence of the great adversary of souls.

3.synthesis: 'Among whom also we all had our conversation in times past in the lusts of our flesh, fulfilling the desires of the flesh and of the mind; and were by nature the children of wrath, even as others' — Paul now associates himself and his fellow Jews with the Gentiles in the same indictment. Calvin explains the pastoral motive: 'Lest it should be supposed that what he had now said was a slanderous reproach against the former character of the Ephesians, or that Jewish pride had led him to treat the Gentiles as an inferior race, he associates himself and his countrymen along with them in the general accusation.' Ellicott notes the movement: 'the great object of this chapter is to bring out the equality and unity of both Jews and Gentiles in the Church of Christ; and this truth is naturally introduced by a statement of their former equality in alienation.' Wesley distinguishes 'flesh' from 'mind': 'flesh denotes the whole evil nature; the mind' (or spirit), its more refined expressions — 'by spiritual, diabolical wickedness.' The phrase 'children of wrath by nature' — a phrase that occupied Augustine, Calvin, and Wesley alike — asserts that the condition of being under divine wrath is not merely a consequence of individual acts but the native state of fallen humanity. This is the anthropological baseline from which ch. 2's gospel arc moves.

voices.html: Lest it should be supposed that what he had now said was a slanderous reproach against the former character of the Ephesians, or that Jewish pride had led him to treat the Gentiles as an inferior race, he associates himself and his countrymen along with them in the general accusation. This is not done in hypocrisy, but in a sincere ascription of glory to God.

Up to this point St. Paul had addressed himself especially to the Ephesians as Gentiles: now he extends the description of alienation to 'all,' Jews and Gentiles alike, as formerly reckoned among the children of disobedience. It is the great object of this chapter to bring out the equality and unity of both in the Church of Christ.

Among whom we — Jews. Also, formerly had our conversation: doing the will of the flesh — In gross, brutal sins. And of the mind — By spiritual, diabolical wickedness. In the former clause, flesh denotes the whole evil nature; in the latter, the mind opposed to the soul. And were by nature — That is, in our natural state. Children of wrath — Having the wrath of God abiding on us, even as the gentiles.

4.synthesis: 'But God, who is rich in mercy, for his great love wherewith he loved us' — the great pivot of the chapter, and one of the most celebrated disjunctions in Paul. After the triple darkness of vv. 1–3 (dead, walking in sin, under wrath), two words reverse everything: 'But God.' Barnes offers the classic comment: 'It is a beautiful expression. God is rich in mercy; overflowing, abundant. Mercy is the riches or the wealth of God. Men are often rich in gold and silver, and they pride themselves in these possessions; but God is rich in mercy.' Clarke sees the pastoral logic: 'as they were corrupt in their nature, and sinful in their practice, they could possess no merit, nor have any claim upon God; and it required much mercy to remove so much misery.' Ellicott notes that the idea of 'richness' in grace, mercy, and glory is especially frequent in this epistle (1:7, 1:18, 2:7, 3:8, 3:16), as if the cosmic scope of the epistle requires correspondingly superlative language for divine generosity. Wesley reduces it to the most compressed form possible: 'Mercy removes misery: love confers salvation.' The verse does not yet complete its sentence — the main verb waits until v. 5 — and the delay itself is rhetorical: the description of God's character precedes the account of his act.

voices.html: It is a beautiful expression. God is rich in mercy; overflowing, abundant. Mercy is the riches or the wealth of God. Men are often rich in gold, and silver, and they pride themselves in these possessions; but God is rich in mercy. In that he abounds; and he is so rich in it he is willing to impart it to others; so rich that he can make all blessed.

Not only merciful, but rich 'in the multitude of mercy,' as attaching even to those dead in sin. The idea of richness in grace, glory, mercy, is especially frequent in this Epistle (see Ephesians 1:7; 1:18; 2:7; 3:8; 3:16). The one moving cause of all God's action is His great love.
Mercy removes misery: love confers salvation.

5.synthesis: 'Even when we were dead in sins, hath quickened us together with Christ (by grace ye are saved)' — the parenthetical aside 'by grace ye are saved' interrupts the main clause precisely because Paul cannot state the fact of the new life without immediately naming its source. Calvin notes the same emphasis as in Romans 5: 'For in due time Christ died for the ungodly... but God commendeth his love toward us, in that, Christ died for us.' Ellicott clarifies the grammatical point: these words should be connected not with 'loved us' but with 'quickened' — 'he brought life out of spiritual death.' The timing matters: God quickened us not after we became worthy, but 'even when we were dead.' Barnes sharpens this: 'it does not mean that he quickened us when we were dead in sin, but that he loved us then' — the pre-temporal love (v. 4) reaches forward to effect the quickening at the moment of conversion. Wesley connects the two grace-notes: 'by grace ye are saved' speaks to both the first instant of regeneration and to every subsequent moment — 'grace is both the beginning and end.' The phrase 'together with Christ' (synezōopoiēsen) ties the believer's spiritual resurrection to Christ's physical resurrection as its ground and pattern, not merely its analogy.

voices.html: These words have the same emphasis as similar expressions in another Epistle: 'For in due time Christ died for the ungodly. But God commendeth his love toward us, in that Christ died for us' (Romans 5:6,8). Whether the words 'by grace ye are saved' have been inserted by another hand, I know not; but, as they are perfectly agreeable to the context, I am willing to receive them as written by Paul.

Even when we were dead in sins — These words should be connected, not with 'loved us,' but with 'hath quickened,' or rather, quickened. He brought life out of spiritual death. The quickening is not in spite of the death, but precisely at the point of the death — while we were yet dead.

He hath quickened us together with Christ — In conformity to him, and by virtue of our union with him. By grace ye are saved — Grace is both the beginning and end. The apostle speaks indifferently either in the first or second person; the Jews and gentiles being in the same circumstance, both by nature and by grace.

6.synthesis: 'And hath raised us up together, and made us sit together in heavenly places in Christ Jesus' — the believer's position in Christ is described proleptically in the past tense (aorist), as if already accomplished, because God's purpose is certain and the spiritual position is real even before its full physical realization. Wesley notes: 'this is spoken by way of anticipation. Believers are not yet possessed of their seats in heaven; but each of them has a place prepared for him.' Clarke reads the verse more presently: 'we have a right to the kingdom of God, anticipate this glory, and are indescribably happy in the foretaste of it.' Barnes understands the seating as a sharing in Christ's honors — 'so close is our connexion with the Redeemer, that his resurrection is connected with ours, and his exaltation with ours' — yet he is careful to call it participatory: believers 'share his honours' but will one day explicitly possess what is now secured. Calvin frames the realized dimension in terms of hope's reality: 'although, as respects ourselves, our salvation is still the object of hope, it is in one sense already possessed in Christ.' The verse anticipates both Ephesians 3:10 (the heavenly display of grace) and 6:12 (the spiritual warfare in heavenly places), giving the believers' seated position in Christ a double significance: security and calling.

voices.html: The resurrection and sitting in heaven, which are here mentioned, are not yet seen by mortal eyes. Yet, as if those blessings were presently in our possession, he states that we have received them. Although, as respects ourselves, our salvation is still the object of hope, it is in one sense already possessed in Christ.

And hath raised us up together — Both Jews and gentiles already in spirit; and ere long our bodies too will be raised. And made us all sit together in heavenly places — This is spoken by way of anticipation. Believers are not yet possessed of their seats in heaven; but each of them has a place prepared for him.

We are raised from the death of sin to the life of religion, in connexion with the resurrection of Jesus, and in virtue of that. So close is the connexion between him and his people, that his resurrection made theirs certain. We share his honours — seated together with him in the heavenly places.

7.synthesis: 'That in the ages to come he might shew the exceeding riches of his grace in his kindness toward us through Christ Jesus' — the purpose of God in raising and seating believers with Christ extends beyond their own blessing to the eternal display of his grace before all creation. Calvin identifies the 'final and true cause' being stated here: the glory of God, to be the subject of earnest study by the Ephesians so they may be 'more fully assured of their salvation.' The word translated 'kindness' (chrēstotēs) carries the nuance of readiness to serve another, of facility in showing good — Ellicott notes it is 'applied to that phase of God's love which delights to minister to His creatures.' Barnes reads the verse as promising that believers' salvation will serve as a perpetual monument of divine mercy across all of history: 'the riches of Divine grace and the Divine benignity would be shown in the conversion of Christians and their salvation to all future times.' Wesley distills it: 'the ages to come — that is, all succeeding ages.' The phrase 'exceeding riches of his grace' resumes the 'riches' language of 1:7, 1:18, 2:4 — suggesting that Ephesians is deliberately building a vocabulary of divine abundance to overwhelm any residual sense that salvation might be a marginal or precarious affair.

voices.html: The ages which are coming on — the ages both of time and of eternity, looked upon in one great continuity. Here the manifestation of the riches of God's grace is looked upon as His special delight, and as His chosen way of manifesting His own self to His creatures. The word 'kindness' properly signifies facility, or readiness to serve another.

The final and true cause — the glory of God — is again mentioned, that the Ephesians, by making it the subject of earnest study, might be more fully assured of their salvation. It was the design of God to hallow, in all ages, the remembrance of so great goodness.

In all future times, the riches of Divine grace and the Divine benignity would be shown in the conversion of Christians and their salvation. Such was his love to those who were lost, that it would be an everlasting monument of his mercy — a perpetual and unchanging proof that he was good.

8.synthesis: 'For by grace are ye saved through faith; and that not of yourselves: it is the gift of God' — this verse, together with v. 9, is the most concentrated statement of Protestant soteriology in the Pauline corpus, and all the voices are in full agreement. Calvin draws the structure precisely: 'God declares that he owes us nothing; so that salvation is not a reward or recompense, but unmixed grace. On one side, we must look at God; and, on the other, at man. God declares that he owes us nothing, and that salvation is a free gift.' The formula 'by grace through faith' assigns the mode (grace as the sphere and source) and the means (faith as the instrument). Wesley catches the symmetry: 'Grace, without any respect to human worthiness, confers the glorious gift. Faith, with an empty hand, and without any pretence to personal desert, receives the heavenly blessing.' Barnes notes this is 'a favourite doctrine with Paul, as it is with all who love the Lord Jesus in sincerity.' Clarke insists on the passive recipient: 'as ye are now brought into a state of salvation, your sins being all blotted out... you must not attribute this to any works or merit of yours.' The phrase 'and that not of yourselves' has been interpreted as referring either to 'faith' specifically (faith is God's gift) or to the whole saving complex — Ellicott and Calvin both favor the latter: the entire salvation is the gift, faith included.

voices.html: This is an inference from the former statements. Having treated of election and of effectual calling, he arrives at this general conclusion, that they had obtained salvation by faith alone. First, he asserts that the salvation of the Ephesians was entirely the work, the gracious work of God. God declares that he owes us nothing; so that salvation is not a reward or recompense, but unmixed grace.

By grace ye are saved through faith — Grace, without any respect to human worthiness, confers the glorious gift. Faith, with an empty hand, and without any pretence to personal desert, receives the heavenly blessing. And this is not of yourselves — This refers to the whole preceding clause, That ye are saved through faith, is the gift of God.

Properly, ye have been saved; ye were saved at first, and continue in a state of salvation. St. Paul now returns to this and works it out, before passing on to draw the conclusion in Ephesians 2:11. The Epistle has been written in the shadow of the controversy with Judaising teachers, and the verse deliberately sums up that controversy: salvation is wholly of grace, wholly received through faith.

By mere favour. It is not by your own merit; it is not because you have any claim. This is a favourite doctrine with Paul, as it is with all who love the Lord Jesus in sincerity. Grace bestowed through faith, or in connexion with believing.

9.synthesis: 'Not of works, lest any man should boast' — the negative corollary of v. 8's positive statement. Works are excluded as a ground of salvation not arbitrarily but because the structure of salvation by grace through faith requires it: if salvation could be merited, it would generate pride rather than praise, and the doxological aim of the whole plan (the praise of God's glory, 1:6, 12, 14) would be defeated. Ellicott reads the verse as 'the echo of the past Judaising controversy; it sums up briefly the whole argument of Romans 3:27 to Romans 4:25.' Calvin notes that Paul always feels as if he has not said enough to forestall the temptation of self-glorying: 'he always feels as if he had not sufficient means of expression; and therefore he adds, after salvation by grace, that it is the gift of God; and after affirming that it is not of ourselves, that it is not of works, lest any man should boast.' Wesley reduces it to the starkest form: 'Not by works — Neither this faith nor this salvation is owing to any works you ever did, will, or can do.' Barnes cites Romans 3:20, 3:27 as the controlling background. The exclusion of boasting is both motivational (removing the temptation) and theological (identifying where all the glory must go).

voices.html: In this verse we have the echo of the past Judaising controversy; it sums up briefly the whole argument of Romans 3:27 to Romans 4:25. There is a similar reminiscence, but more distinct and detached, in Philippians 3:2–9. The exclusion of works is not arbitrary but flows from the inner logic of salvation by grace.

Instead of what he had said, that their salvation is of grace, he now affirms, that 'it is the gift of God,' and after affirming that it is not of ourselves, he adds that it is not of works, lest any man should boast. He always feels as if he had not sufficient means of expression; and therefore he keeps repeating and amplifying the same thought.
Not by works — Neither this faith nor this salvation is owing to any works you ever did, will, or can do.

10.synthesis: 'For we are his workmanship, created in Christ Jesus unto good works, which God hath before ordained that we should walk in them' — the verse resolves any misunderstanding that the exclusion of works (v. 9) means works are irrelevant to the Christian life. Works are not the root but they are the fruit. Ellicott notes the rare word translated 'workmanship' (poiēma) is used only here and in Romans 1:20 (of God's creation works), suggesting the same creative power that made the visible world is at work in the new creation of believers — 'this verse is unique and remarkable, characteristic of the idea with which this Epistle starts — the election and predestination of God, making us what we are.' Calvin states the principle plainly: 'by setting aside the contrary supposition, he proves his statement — we have no remaining works by which we can merit salvation; for all the good works which we possess are the fruit of regeneration. Hence it follows, that works themselves are a part of grace.' Wesley draws the pastoral implication: created 'unto good works — that afterwards we might give ourselves to them.' The 'before ordained' (proētoimasen) means God prepared occasions for good works in advance, so that even the works are traced back to divine preparation, not human initiative. Barnes: 'whatever of peace, or hope, or purity we have, has been produced by his agency on the soul.'

voices.html: This verse, on the contrary, is unique and remarkable, characteristic of the idea with which this Epistle starts — the election and predestination of God, making us what we are — and applying it very strikingly, not only to the first regeneration, but even to the good works which follow it. The word rendered 'workmanship' is only used elsewhere in Romans 1:20, where it is applied to the 'works' of God in creation.

By setting aside the contrary supposition, he proves his statement — that we have no remaining works by which we can merit salvation; for all the good works which we possess are the fruit of regeneration. Hence it follows, that works themselves are a part of grace. When he says that 'we are the work of God,' this does not refer to ordinary creation, but we are declared to be new creatures, because not by our own power but by the Spirit of God we have been formed for righteousness.

For we are his workmanship — Which proves both that salvation is by faith, and that faith is the gift of God. Created unto good works — That afterwards we might give ourselves to them. Which God had before prepared — The occasions of them: so we must still ascribe the whole to God. That we might walk in them — Though not be justified by them.

11.synthesis: 'Wherefore remember that ye being in time past Gentiles in the flesh, who are called Uncircumcision by that which is called the Circumcision in the flesh made by hands' — Paul pivots to the second major section of the chapter (vv. 11–22), which draws out the Jewish-Gentile reconciliation in the church. The exhortation to 'remember' is pastoral strategy, not mere nostalgia: Barnes observes that 'no faculty of the mind can be better employed to produce humility, penitence, gratitude, and love, than the memory; it is well to recall the recollection of our former sins.' Wesley notes that such remembrance 'strengthens faith and increases gratitude.' The Gentiles are identified by the Jewish name for them — 'Uncircumcision' — which Ellicott reads with a touch of the contempt implied in 'the so-called' — simply implying that now both Circumcision and Uncircumcision are 'mere names, virtually nothing.' The parenthetical qualifier 'in the flesh made by hands' prepares for the contrast to come: the true circumcision is of the heart (Romans 2:29), and the bodily sign that once divided is now rendered null by Christ's work. Clarke notes the Gentiles' former condition pointedly: 'heathens in the flesh, under the influence of their corrupt nature.'

voices.html: The design of this evidently is to excite a sense of gratitude in their bosoms for that mercy which had called them from the errors and sins of their former lives to the privileges of Christians. It is a good thing for Christians to 'remember' what they were. No faculty of the mind can be better employed to produce humility, penitence, gratitude, and love, than the memory.

The use of the phrase 'called' — with a touch of the contempt implied in our phrase 'the so-called' — simply implies that now Circumcision and Uncircumcision were mere names, virtually 'nothing.' The declaration of the nullity of circumcision as a religious distinction is now taken for granted without argument.

Wherefore remember — Such a remembrance strengthens faith, and increases gratitude. That ye being formerly gentiles in the flesh — Neither circumcised in body nor in spirit. Who were accordingly called the uncircumcision — By way of reproach.

12.synthesis: 'That at that time ye were without Christ, aliens from the commonwealth of Israel, and strangers from the covenants of promise, having no hope, and without God in the world' — Paul accumulates five descriptions of Gentile destitution, each more bleak than the last. Ellicott calls this 'a dark and terrible picture of the former heathen condition of the Ephesians, intentionally contrasted in every point with the description of Christian privilege in Ephesians 2:19–20.' Barnes anchors the analysis in the first phrase: 'nothing would better express their condition than to say they were 'without Christ' — they knew of no atonement for sin, no advocate before God, no ground of hope in a life to come.' Calvin describes the cumulative effect: the Ephesians 'had been excluded, not only from the outward badge, but from everything necessary to the salvation and happiness of men.' Wesley unpacks 'without God': 'wholly ignorant of the true God, and so in effect atheists — such in truth are, more or less, all men without God in the world.' Clarke notes that 'alienated from the commonwealth of Israel' means they were 'by birth, idolatry, etc., alienated from the civil and religious privileges of the Jewish people.' Strangers to the covenants means no access to the promises made to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob — the whole prophetic horizon was shut to them. The result: 'having no hope' in any grounded, gospel sense.

voices.html: This verse gives a dark and terrible picture of the former heathen condition of the Ephesians, intentionally contrasted in every point with the description of Christian privilege in Ephesians 2:19–20. That condition is first summed up in one expression: 'separate from Christ.' Then from this are drawn two gloomy consequences: alienation from Israel's commonwealth, and estrangement from the covenants of promise.

Ye were without Christ — You were without the knowledge of the Messiah. You had not heard of him; of course you had not embraced him. Nothing would better express their condition than to say they were 'without Christ' — they knew of no atonement for sin, no advocate before God, no ground of hope in a life to come.

Were at that time without Christ — Having no faith in, or knowledge of, him. Being aliens from the commonwealth of Israel — Both as to their temporal privileges and spiritual blessings. And strangers to the covenants of promise — The great promise in both the Jewish and Christian covenant was the Messiah. Having no hope — Because they had no promise whereon to ground their hope. And being without God — Wholly ignorant of the true God, and so in effect atheists.

13.synthesis: 'But now in Christ Jesus ye who sometimes were far off are made nigh by the blood of Christ' — the second great reversal of the chapter (echoing 'but God' in v. 4). The language of far and near derives from Jewish usage: to be near the temple was to be near God; Gentiles were 'far off' in the most comprehensive sense — geographically, covenantally, spiritually. Barnes traces the idiom: 'God was supposed to reside in the temple. Those who were remote from Jerusalem were said to be far off; those who were near were said to be nigh.' Clarke adds the prophetic background (citing Isaiah): in the Old Testament, Jews who fell under divine displeasure could also be said to be 'far off.' Now the instrument of nearness is specified unambiguously: 'the blood of Christ' — not moral improvement, not cultural adoption, not proselyte conversion, but the atoning death. Ellicott frames the transition christologically: the restoration takes place first 'in Christ Jesus' — in virtue of union with Him through all the acts of His mediation; and next 'by the blood of Christ' — that is, through that especial act of mediation which is emphatically an atonement for sin. Wesley compresses it to four words: 'Far off — from God and his people. Nigh — intimately united to both.'

voices.html: This verse speaks of the restoration of the heathen as taking place, first, 'in Christ Jesus' — in virtue of union with Him through all the acts of His mediation; and next, 'by the blood of Christ' — that is, through that especial act of mediation which is emphatically an atonement for sin — such sin as St. Paul had been declaring above to be the cause of spiritual deadness.

The phrase 'far off' means that they were formerly far off from God and his people. The expression is derived from the custom of speaking among the Hebrews. God was supposed to reside in the temple. Those who were remote from Jerusalem were said to be far off; those who were near were said to be nigh. The Gentiles were regarded as 'afar off' in every sense.

To be far off, and to be near, are sayings much in use among the Jews; and among them, to be near signifies to be in the approbation or favor of God; and to be far off signifies to be under his displeasure. So a wicked Jew might be said to be far off from God; and a holy man, or a genuine penitent, might be said to be nigh to God, because such persons are in his favor.

14.synthesis: 'For he is our peace, who hath made both one, and hath broken down the middle wall of partition between us' — Christ is not merely the author of peace or its proclaimer but, as Ellicott stresses, 'only here is our Lord called not the giver of peace, but the peace itself — his own nature being the actual meeting-place and reconciliation of the two.' This is one of the epistle's most striking christological statements. Calvin calls it 'a beautiful title' and notes that if Christ is all who are out of him must be at variance with God and one another. Barnes identifies the Isaiah 57:19 allusion and notes that the peace described is specifically the union in worship produced between Jews and Gentiles. Clarke explains the 'middle wall of partition' with historical concreteness: the barrier in the temple court separating the Court of the Gentiles from the Court of Israel, with posted warnings that any non-Jew who crossed it would be put to death — 'by abolishing the law of Jewish ordinances, he has removed that which kept the two parties not only in a state of separation, but also at variance.' The making of 'both one' refers first to the historical Jew-Gentile reconciliation and, through it, to the reconciliation of all humanity with God that is elaborated in vv. 15–18.

voices.html: Here, however, only is our Lord called not the giver of peace, but the peace itself — His own nature being the actual meeting-place and reconciliation of the two. There is clearly allusion to the many promises in the Old Testament of the 'Prince of Peace' (Isaiah 9:5–6), so still more to the 'Peace of Earth' of the angelic song of Bethlehem, and to the repeated declarations of our Lord, 'Peace I leave with you: My peace I give unto you.'

Jesus Christ has died for both Jews and Gentiles, and has become a peace-offering, shalom, to reconcile both to God and to each other. Who hath made both one — Formed one Church out of the believers of both people. The middle wall of partition — By abolishing the law of Jewish ordinances, he has removed that which kept the two parties, not only in a state of separation, but also at variance.

What a beautiful title is this which Christ possesses — the peace between God and men, between Jews and Gentiles! He now includes Jews in the privilege of reconciliation, and shows that, through one Messiah, all are united to God. If Christ is all who are out of him must be at variance with God and with each other.

15.synthesis: 'Having abolished in his flesh the enmity, even the law of commandments contained in ordinances; for to make in himself of twain one new man, so making peace' — the verse identifies what precisely was 'abolished' (katargeō — rendered null and void) in Christ's incarnation and death: the law of commandments in ordinances, specifically the ceremonial requirements that distinguished Jew from Gentile and maintained a legal wall between them. Calvin argues that the ceremonies by which the distinction was declared have been superseded — 'the Son of God, by assuming a nature common to all, has formed in his own body a perfect unity.' Clarke identifies the 'enmity' as reciprocal: 'the Jews detested the Gentiles, and the Gentiles despised the Jews' — Christ's death ended both hostilities simultaneously. Barnes carefully uses katargeō: 'having brought to naught or put an end to it' — and locates the instrument in the 'flesh' of Christ, meaning not instruction or example but the sacrifice of his body on the cross. The purpose clause is crucial: the aim is not merely the removal of something bad but the positive creation of 'one new man' — a new humanity in Christ that transcends the old ethnic and legal categories. Ellicott notes the difficult grammar (whether 'enmity' is in apposition to the 'wall' of v. 14 or to 'law of commandments') but says 'the general sense is but little affected in either case.'

voices.html: The ceremonies, by which the distinction was declared, have been superseded. The reason why it is broken down is now added — by the flesh of Christ. The Son of God, by assuming a nature common to all, has formed in his own body a perfect unity. What had been metaphorically understood by the word 'wall' is now more plainly expressed.

Having abolished — Having brought to naught or put an end to it — katargeō — In his flesh. By the sacrifice of his body on the cross. It was not by instruction merely; it was not by communicating the knowledge of God; it was by his flesh — his human nature — and this can mean only that he did it by his sacrifice of himself.

Having abolished in his flesh — By his incarnation and death he not only made an atonement for sin, but he appointed the doctrine of reconciliation to God, and of love to each other, to be preached in all nations. The enmity of which the apostle speaks was reciprocal among the Jews and Gentiles. The former detested the Gentiles, and the Gentiles despised the Jews; Christ's death ended both hostilities simultaneously.

16.synthesis: 'And that he might reconcile both unto God in one body by the cross, having slain the enmity thereby' — the dimension of the atonement shifts here from horizontal (Jew-Gentile reconciliation) to vertical (reconciliation of both to God). Calvin insists this is 'not the only advantage which we derive from Christ'; the reconciliation between formerly divided people is real but secondary to the fact that 'we have been brought back into favor with God.' Barnes makes the point most precisely: 'it was not merely to make them harmonious, but it was that both, who had been alienated from God, should be reconciled to him — this was a different effect from that of producing peace between themselves, though in some sense the one grew out of the other.' The phrase 'in one body' is often read as referring to the church (one body = the one corporate body of believers), but both Ellicott and Calvin read 'in his body' — in Christ's own crucified body the reconciliation was achieved and both parties were united. Ellicott's Greek note: the word used here (apokatallassō) is a compound unique to Colossians 1:20–21 and Ephesians 2:16 — meaning not merely 'to conciliate' but 'to re-unite those who were originally united but afterwards separated.' The enmity slain is the opposition of a rebellious humanity to a holy God, ended by the substitutionary death of the cross.

voices.html: The reconciliation between ourselves which has now been described is not the only advantage which we derive from Christ. We have been brought back into favor with God. The Jews are thus led to consider that they have not less need of a Mediator than the Gentiles. Without this, neither the Law, nor ceremonies, nor descent from Abraham, nor all their dazzling prerogatives, would be of any avail.

On the reconciliation of man to God, see the great passage 2 Corinthians 5:18–21. It should be noted that the word used here and in Colossians 1:20–21 is a compound signifying not simply to 'conciliate,' but properly to 'reconcile,' — that is, to reunite those who were originally united, but afterwards separated by sin and rebellion.

This was another of the effects of the work of redemption, and indeed the main effect. It was not merely to make them harmonious, but it was that both, who had been alienated from God, should be reconciled to him. This was a different effect from that of producing peace between themselves, though in some sense the one grew out of the other — they who are reconciled to God will be at peace with each other.

17.synthesis: 'And came and preached peace to you which were afar off, and to them that were nigh' — having accomplished reconciliation by his death, Christ came and proclaimed it. Ellicott connects 'came' specifically to the resurrection appearances, noting that the word points back to our Lord's own preaching when, after the Resurrection, He came and stood in the midst of them and said 'Peace be unto you' (Luke 24:36; John 20:19, 21). At those moments Christ himself was the peace-preacher, and then sent the disciples to continue the proclamation: 'As my Father hath sent me, even so send I you.' Calvin draws out the logical necessity: 'All that Christ had done towards effecting a reconciliation would have been of no service, if it had not been proclaimed by the gospel; and therefore he adds that the fruit of this peace has now been offered both to Jews and to Gentiles.' Barnes distinguishes the modes of preaching: Christ preached personally to 'those who were nigh' (the Jews) and through his apostles to 'those who were afar off' (the Gentiles) — though he was the author of the system that proclaimed salvation to both. Wesley adds: 'And he came — after his resurrection — and preached peace — by his ministers and his Spirit.' The Isaiah 57:19 echo ('Peace, peace, to him that is far off, and to him that is near') ties Christ's proclamation to a specific prophetic promise now fulfilled.

voices.html: The word 'came' certainly carries back our thoughts to our Lord's own preaching, when, after the Resurrection, He came 'and stood in the midst of them, and said, Peace be unto you' (Luke 24:36; John 20:19, 21). But at that very time He repeated the salutation 'Peace be unto you,' with the expressive addition, 'As my Father hath sent Me, even so send I you.'

All that Christ had done towards effecting a reconciliation would have been of no service, if it had not been proclaimed by the gospel; and therefore he adds, that the fruit of this peace has now been offered both to Jews and to Gentiles. Hence it follows, that to save Gentiles as well as Jews was the design of our Savior's coming, as the preaching of the gospel, which is addressed indiscriminately to both, makes abundantly manifest.

That is, the system of religion which he proclaimed was adapted to produce peace with God. This he preached personally to those who 'were nigh,' that is, the Jews; to those who were 'afar off' — the Gentiles — he preached it by his apostles. He was the Author of the system which proclaimed salvation to both.

18.synthesis: 'For through him we both have access by one Spirit unto the Father' — the verse states the Trinitarian structure of prayer and approach to God in its most compact form. Clarke calls it 'a plain proof of the holy Trinity: Jews and Gentiles are to be presented unto God the Father; the Spirit of God works in their hearts and prepares them for this presentation; and Jesus Christ himself introduces them.' The access (prosagōgē) is not self-generated but granted — Ellicott identifies prosagōgē as a technical word for 'the introduction' (used also in Ephesians 3:12; Romans 5:2) — the formal granting of entry into a royal or divine presence. Barnes specifies three modes by which Christ secured this access: by his death (reconciling us to God), by his teaching (acquainting us with God), and by his intercession in heaven. Both Jews and Gentiles now approach the same Father, through the same Son, by the same Spirit — the one Spirit dissolving any claim that one group has a privileged avenue of access. Calvin sees the verse as announcing the true nature of evangelical peace: not the false quiet of those who 'lull themselves into a profound sleep... by keeping themselves at the greatest possible distance from God,' but the confident approach of those who have been reconciled. Wesley: 'Christ, the Spirit, and the Father, the three-one God, stand frequently in the same order.'

voices.html: For through him — Christ Jesus — we both — Jews and Gentiles — have access by one Spirit — through the influence of the Holy Ghost — unto the Father — God Almighty. This text is a plain proof of the holy Trinity. Jews and Gentiles are to be presented unto God the Father; the Spirit of God works in their hearts, and prepares them for this presentation; and Jesus Christ himself introduces them.

In the original the order is emphatic: 'Through Him we have the access, both of us in one Spirit, to the Father.' The greater idea of access to God is still prominent; but the lesser idea of union with each other in that access is still traceable as an undertone. 'Access' is properly 'the introduction' — a technical word used also in Ephesians 3:12; Romans 5:2.

Through him — that is, he has secured this result that we have access to God. This he did by his death — reconciling us to God; by the doctrines which he taught — acquainting us with God; and by his intercession in heaven — by which our prayers gain acceptance with him. We both have access — both Jews and Gentiles — to God through him.

19.synthesis: 'Now therefore ye are no more strangers and foreigners, but fellowcitizens with the saints, and of the household of God' — three ascending metaphors mark the Gentiles' transformed status. Ellicott is precise about the Greek: 'stranger' (xenos) means a foreigner in a state, with no civic rights; 'foreigner' (paroikos) means a resident alien — one who lives among citizens but has only partial rights, 'half-aliens.' Now the Ephesian Gentiles are neither: they are polítai (full citizens) and oikeioi (household members). The move from citizenship to household membership is significant — Barnes notes that 'strangers and foreigners' refer to positions in a state, while 'household of God' refers to family intimacy. Calvin identifies three metaphors in vv. 19–22 through which Paul expresses the Ephesians' honor: members of a holy state, members of God's family, and stones in the temple of the Lord. Clarke extends the civic metaphor: formerly, 'when any of them came to Jerusalem, being strangers, they had no kind of rights whatever; nor could they, as mere heathens, settle among them.' Wesley reduces to the essential: 'ye are no longer strangers, but citizens of the heavenly Jerusalem; no longer foreigners, but received into the very family of God.' The verse marks the end of the alienation described in v. 12 — each element of the old destitution now has its reversal.

voices.html: Here the word rendered 'stranger' means properly an alien, or foreigner; while the word translated 'foreigners' signifies the resident aliens of an ancient city, who were but half-aliens, having free intercourse with the citizens, although no rights of citizenship. Such were, for instance, the resident 'strangers' at Athens who paid tribute but had no political rights.

Ye are reckoned with the people of God. You are entitled to their privileges, and are not to be regarded as outcasts and aliens. The word rendered 'strangers' means foreigners in a state, as opposed to citizens. The word rendered 'foreigners' means guests in a private family, as opposed to the members of the family — now they are full members of both the city and the household.
Therefore ye are no longer strangers, but citizens of the heavenly Jerusalem; no longer foreigners, but received into the very family of God.

20.synthesis: 'And are built upon the foundation of the apostles and prophets, Jesus Christ himself being the chief corner stone' — the third metaphor completes the picture: the Ephesian believers are not merely citizens of God's city and members of his family but living stones in his temple. The identity of the 'prophets' here has divided commentators. Ellicott makes the strongest case for NT prophets: 'in spite of much ancient and valuable authority, it seems impossible to take the prophets of this verse to be the prophets of the Old Testament — the order of the two words and the comparison of Ephesians 3:5; Ephesians 4:11 appear to be decisive.' In those passages prophets are listed alongside apostles as contemporary offices in the church. Calvin, Clarke, and Barnes all read 'apostles and prophets' as referring to OT prophets paired with NT apostles — the continuity of the one word of God across both testaments. Calvin insists the church's foundation is 'founded on the doctrine' — apostolic and prophetic doctrine, not on the persons themselves. Wesley: 'the word of God, declared by the apostles and prophets, sustains the faith of all believers.' All voices agree that Christ himself is the chief cornerstone — the one without whom no building stands — and that his position at the corner means he holds together walls that would otherwise diverge.

voices.html: In spite of much ancient and valuable authority, it seems impossible to take 'the prophets' of this verse to be the prophets of the Old Testament. The order of the two words and the comparison of Ephesians 3:5; Ephesians 4:11 appear to be decisive — in both passages, 'apostles and prophets' refer to contemporary offices in the church, not to OT predecessors.

The third comparison illustrates the manner in which the Ephesians, and all other Christians, are admitted to this honor. They are founded on the doctrine of apostles and prophets. We are thus enabled to distinguish between a true and a false church: this is of the greatest importance, for the tendency to error is always strong, and the consequences of mistake are dangerous in the extreme.

And are built upon the foundation of the apostles and prophets — As the foundation sustains the building, so the word of God, declared by the apostles and prophets, sustains the faith of all believers. God laid the foundation by them; but Christ himself is the chief corner-stone of the foundation. Elsewhere he is termed the foundation itself (1 Corinthians 3:11).

20.key_tension: Ellicott reads 'prophets' as NT prophets contemporary with the apostles (citing Eph 3:5; 4:11), while Calvin, Clarke, Barnes, and Wesley read them as OT prophets, treating 'apostles and prophets' as the two-testament witness on which the church is founded.

21.synthesis: 'In whom all the building fitly framed together groweth unto an holy temple in the Lord' — the metaphor of the temple now becomes dynamic: not a finished building but a growing one, as new believers are added and existing ones mature. Ellicott notes a textual difficulty — whether to read 'all the building' (with the article) or 'every act of building' (without it) — and concludes that the clause agrees substantially with either rendering: the point is that in Christ every element of the construction is properly joined and bonded. Clarke: 'it is not a finished building, but will continue to increase, and be more and more perfect, till the day of judgment.' Wesley adds a characteristically pointed comparison for his Ephesian audience: 'What is the temple of Diana of the Ephesians, whom ye formerly worshipped, to this?' — the greatest ancient wonder of the world is nothing against the living temple of God in his church. Calvin notes that the verse further distinguishes true from false churches: the true church grows because it is fitly framed in Christ, the cornerstone; a building erected on any other foundation does not grow toward holiness but toward ruin. Barnes explains 'fitly framed together' (sunarmologoumenē): to joint together as a carpenter does the frame-work of a building — 'different materials may be used, and different kinds of timber may be employed; but one part shall be accurately fitted to another.'

voices.html: On whom all the building fitly framed together — The whole fabric of the universal church rises up like a great pile of living materials. Into an holy temple in the Lord — Dedicated to Christ, and inhabited by him, in which he displays his presence, and is worshipped and glorified. What is the temple of Diana of the Ephesians, whom ye formerly worshipped, to this?

In whom — by which foundation corner stone, Christ Jesus — all the building, composed of converted Jews and Gentiles, fitly framed together, properly jointed and connected together, groweth unto a holy temple — is continually increasing, as new converts from Judaism or heathenism flock into it. It is not a finished building, but will continue to increase, and be more and more perfect, till the day of judgment.

Fitly framed together — The word here used means, to joint together, as a carpenter does the frame-work of a building. The materials are accurately and carefully united by mortices and tenons, so that the building shall be firm. Different materials may be used, and different kinds of timber may be employed; but one part shall be accurately fitted to another, so that the whole shall be compact and firm.

22.synthesis: 'In whom ye also are builded together for an habitation of God through the Spirit' — the chapter closes by turning the great architectural metaphor back onto the Ephesians themselves: they are not merely spectators of God's temple-building project but active participants and constituent stones in it. Ellicott reads the verse as primarily emphasizing the individual-to-corporate dynamic: 'each stone being itself a complete and living unity with Christ' even as it forms part of a larger whole — the church is not merely a collective but a body in which each member has a direct, living relation to the cornerstone. Calvin debates the Greek verb form (passive or imperative, indicative or exhortation) and resolves for the indicative: it is 'a statement of fact and an exhortation to grow more and more in the faith of Christ.' Barnes stresses the relational language: 'for the indwelling, or the dwelling-place, of God' — formerly God dwelt in the temple at Jerusalem; now he dwells in the church and in the hearts of his people (citing 2 Corinthians 6:16). Clarke returns to the building metaphor: as stones in a temple are properly placed to form a complete house for the Deity, so believers are 'prepared by the doctrine of the prophets and apostles, under the influence of the Spirit of God, to be the dwelling place of the Most High.' The chapter's whole arc — from spiritual death, through divine initiative, to glorious destination — ends not with a creed but a dwelling: God at home in his people.

voices.html: This verse seems primarily intended to emphasise the truth already enunciated, that the Ephesians themselves are now being made part of the Church of Christ, 'being built up together in Christ.' But it may also illustrate the character of the unity of the Church, as primarily a direct individual unity with Christ — each stone being itself a complete and living unity with Christ even as it forms part of the whole.

For an habitation of God — For the indwelling, or the dwelling-place, of God. Formerly he dwelt in the temple at Jerusalem; now he dwells in the church, and in the hearts of his people (2 Corinthians 6:16). You are not merely added to it, but you constitute a part of the building itself — integral stones in the holy habitation.

As the stones in a temple are all properly placed so as to form a complete house, and be a habitation for the Deity that is worshipped there, so ye are all, both believing Jews and Gentiles, prepared by the doctrine of the prophets and apostles, under the influence of the Spirit of God, to be the dwelling place of the Most High.

1.synthesis:
The opening command of chapter 5 is one of the boldest in the New Testament: be imitators of God, as beloved children. Paul has already grounded the ethical exhortations of chapter 4 in the character of God; now he makes the implication explicit. The standard for Christian conduct is not social custom or even religious law but the character of the Father himself. Ellicott notes that the phrase imitators of God is unique in biblical literature—direct, unqualified, and anchored in the logic of family resemblance. As children bear the image of their father, so God's children are to bear his moral character.

Calvin explains that we are reminded of our sonship so that we will pursue the likeness of our Father—not as an impossible ideal but as the natural expression of what we have become by adoption. Wesley draws out the wonder of it: to be an imitator of God is an honor exceeding anything the ancient world offered, whether imitating Homer, Virgil, or the greatest conquerors. Barnes sets the verse in its immediate context: the chapter is a continuation of the practical exhortations of chapter 4, and the prime attribute of God to be imitated is the love shown in the sacrifice of Christ that follows in verse 2.

voices.html: We are reminded that we are the children of God, and that therefore we ought, as far as possible, to resemble him. As a child is naturally inclined to imitate his father, so those adopted into God's family are to reproduce his character. The chief attribute to imitate, as verse 2 makes clear, is love—the love that gives without calculation and forgives without reservation.

The phrase imitators of God is unique and very striking. The word therefore implies that this imitation of God must be chiefly in his essential attribute of love. It is instructive that our Lord's statement in Matthew 5:48—be perfect as your Father in heaven is perfect—is given in the context of love for enemies, which is exactly the theme Paul develops here.

Be imitators of God in forgiving and loving. O how much more honourable and more happy, to be an imitator of God, than of Homer, Virgil, or Alexander the Great! The world sets up human excellence as the model; Paul sets up the divine character—specifically the character of a Father who loves freely and completely.

2.synthesis:
Verse 2 grounds the call to imitate God's love in the concrete example of Christ's self-giving: walk in love, as Christ loved us and gave himself up for us, a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God. The imagery of the fragrant offering is drawn from the Levitical sacrifice system (Exodus 29:18; Leviticus 1:9), where offerings well-performed were described as a pleasing aroma to God. Paul applies that language to Christ's death: the entire life of self-giving love, culminating in the cross, was the perfect sacrifice that satisfied God's requirements.

Calvin stresses that we ought to embrace one another with the same love with which Christ has embraced us—his love is not merely an example to admire but our actual model. What we perceive in Christ is our true guide. Ellicott notes that the verb gave himself up implies voluntary surrender; it was an act of will, not a fate suffered unwillingly. Barnes emphasizes that the command is to walk in love—to let love be the atmosphere of one's life, the quality that characterizes all conduct and relationships, not an occasional act of kindness but a sustained disposition flowing from gratitude for Christ's sacrifice.

voices.html: We ought to embrace each other with the love with which Christ has embraced us, for what we perceive in Christ is our true model. This love sacrificed itself—it gave, it did not merely feel warmly. The perfume of the offering rises to God precisely because it is genuine self-giving, and that is what Paul calls believers to reproduce in their daily relationships.

Paul adds to the call to imitate God the example of our Lord Jesus Christ in that especial exhibition of love expressed through suffering and self-sacrifice. The verb gave himself up implies voluntary surrender—Christ was not a passive victim but an active giver. His entire life of love, consummated on the cross, was the fragrant offering that pleased God and models what love looks like among believers.

Walk in love—let your lives be characterized by love, evinced in all your deportment and conversation. We are to show the same love for one another which Christ showed for us. He gave himself for us as a sweet-smelling sacrifice, an offering of infinite worth because it was entirely voluntary and entirely pure. This is the standard for the love Paul commands.

3.synthesis:
Having set the highest possible standard—love that imitates Christ—Paul immediately names what contradicts it: sexual immorality, all impurity, and covetousness are not to be even named among believers. The phrase is striking. Paul does not merely say these things are to be avoided; he says they should not even be part of the church's vocabulary as acceptable options. To name them as live possibilities would already be a betrayal of the community's character.

Calvin notes the three vices correspond to parallel passage in Colossians 3 and cluster around bodily and material excess—sins of flesh and greed that flow from placing the body's appetites above God. Clarke explains that the three terms may be different species of the same general category of impurity: porneia including not just fornication but adultery and related sexual sins, akatharsia covering the broader field of impurity, and pleonexia (covetousness) named alongside because an excessive, grasping attitude toward possessions mirrors the grasping character of sexual vice. Wesley cuts to the point: keep the utmost distance from anything impure—even the language of toleration corrupts the atmosphere of holiness.

voices.html: Three things are here enumerated which the apostle declares to be unworthy of mention among Christians: sexual immorality, all impurity, and covetousness. These are not merely forbidden acts but a category of life incompatible with the family of God. To not be named means they should have no recognized place, no acceptable status, no toleration even in conversation among those who bear the name of Christ.

The three terms used here probably refer to different species of the same general disorder. Porneia may imply not only fornication but adultery and related sins. Akatharsia covers the broader field of moral impurity. Pleonexia—covetousness—is named alongside because greed is a form of the same grasping, self-centered spirit that underlies sexual sin. All three are to be so foreign to Christian life that they have no name among believers.

Let not any impure love be even named or heard of among you—keep at the utmost distance from it, as becomes saints. The standard is not avoid these when you can but maintain such an atmosphere of holiness that these things have no recognized existence among you. Even the tolerance of naming them as live options for believers corrupts the community's character.

4.synthesis:
Three more vices extend the catalogue from acts to words: filthiness, foolish talking, and jesting, none of which is fitting, but instead let there be thanksgiving. The antithesis is deliberate and instructive: the opposite of indecent, empty, or cleverly mocking speech is not solemn silence but grateful praise. A heart occupied with gratitude to God does not easily fill its mouth with obscenity or frivolity.

Calvin defines aischrotes (filthiness) as anything indecent or inconsistent with the modesty of the godly, and morologia (foolish talking) as conversation that is either unprofitably or wickedly foolish. Clarke gives the sharpest definitions: morologia is scurrility and buffoonery that exposes others to contempt, while eutrapelia (jesting) is artfully turned discourse that twists serious things to ridicule. Wesley adds a striking observation about jesting: it was prized by the Greeks as a social virtue, almost a half-virtue; but even this approved refinement, in experience, quenches the Spirit and deadens the soul to sacred things. Barnes notes that these three words are rare in the New Testament, suggesting Paul reached for specific terms to name specific cultural practices he knew his readers would recognize.

voices.html: By filthiness Paul understands all that is indecent or inconsistent with the modesty of the godly. By foolish talking he means conversations either unprofitably or wickedly foolish. By jesting he means clever speech that turns serious things into ridicule. All three misuse the tongue that should be engaged in thanksgiving, and all three are incompatible with the life of one who has received the gift of God in Christ.

Filthiness covers anything base or vile in words or acts. Foolish talking means scurrility, buffoonery, and ridicule—speech that exposes another to contempt. Jesting is artfully turned discourse that twists what is serious or sacred into an occasion for laughter. All three are substitutes for the only speech that truly fits a redeemed person: thanksgiving to God for what Christ has done.

Jesting—the Greek word means wittiness, facetiousness—was esteemed by the heathens as almost a virtue. But how frequently even this quenches the Spirit! Those who are tender of conscience know from experience how laughter at sacred or moral things dulls the sensitivity to God, and how easily what begins as wit ends in the corruption of the whole conversation.

5.synthesis:
Paul sharpens the warning with a stark declaration about the consequences of the vices just listed: everyone who is sexually immoral or impure, or who is covetous (that is, an idolater), has no inheritance in the kingdom of Christ and God. The identification of the covetous person as an idolater is theologically precise: covetousness is not merely a financial disorder but a displacement of God from the center of one's trust and desire by a created thing. Whatever we most want, most fear losing, and most orient our lives toward has become our functional god—and for the covetous person, that god is wealth or what it secures.

Calvin explains that Paul alarms his readers with the gravity of these sins so that they will lend an earnest rather than careless ear to his admonitions. Barnes notes that the purpose is to deter by the solemn assurance that no one who commits these things can possibly be saved, not to induce despair but to produce genuine seriousness about the danger. Clarke observes that all Paul's readers could perceive the ruinous tendency of such conduct when they understood that persons of this character are excluded from the kingdom of God—a kingdom Paul has spent the preceding chapters describing as their highest calling and inheritance.

voices.html: Paul determines to alarm his readers so that they will lend an earnest rather than careless ear to his admonitions. The vices of verses 3–4 are not minor blemishes but disqualifications from the kingdom. The covetous person is called an idolater because covetousness is a form of worship—it places a created thing at the center where God alone belongs, and in doing so excludes the worshipper from the inheritance of God's children.

Paul's readers must be convinced of the dangerous tendency of such conduct when they know that persons of this character can never inherit the kingdom of God. The covetous man is called an idolater because his heart is given to riches as the supreme good, which is what God alone has a right to claim. The exclusion from the kingdom is not a threat to produce fear but a statement of what the kingdom actually is and who can enter it.

The object here is to deter from indulgence in these vices by the solemn assurance that no one who commits them can be saved. This is not said to produce despair but to produce serious, honest reckoning with what is at stake. Covetousness is identified as idolatry because it gives to wealth the trust, love, and supreme value that belong only to God—and idolatry, in any form, cannot inherit the kingdom.

6.synthesis:
Let no one deceive you with empty words, for because of these things the wrath of God comes upon the sons of disobedience. Paul anticipates the sophistry that was certainly circulating: voices that minimized the gravity of the sins just named, whether by appealing to Gentile cultural norms, to a philosophical divorce of the body from the soul, or to an early form of the antinomian Gnosticism that later taught that what the body does is spiritually irrelevant. Against all such rationalizations Paul sets the plain fact of divine wrath.

Ellicott notes that Paul seems to have in view not merely worldly condonation of evil but some anticipation of the Antinomian form of Gnosticism that held that actions done in the body being material do not affect the soul. Calvin observes that in all ages Satan raises up those who make the threatenings of God the subject of merriment and ridicule; the Ephesians are to resist them. Clarke notes that those who practice such things are already under the wrath of God, and to be deceived into thinking these things innocent or unavoidable is to be deceived into sharing their condemnation. Barnes warns that the Ephesians were under strong temptation to join in festive scenes where these vices were not frowned on—social pressure from the surrounding culture was the specific danger Paul addresses.

voices.html: Paul likely has in view not merely worldly tolerance of evil but some anticipation of the Antinomian Gnosticism that taught actions done in the body do not affect the soul. Against this sophisticated rationalization, he states the unqualified fact: the wrath of God comes upon the sons of disobedience for exactly these things. Sophistication does not neutralize divine judgment—it only obscures the danger until it is too late to turn.

Suffer no man to persuade you that any of these things are innocent, or that they are unavoidable frailties of human nature. They are sins and abominations in the sight of God, and those who practice them are already under his wrath. The person who deceives you about this shares the guilt and incurs the same condemnation as those actively engaged in the evil.

The Ephesians would be under strong temptations to mingle in the gay and festive scenes where these vices were not frowned on. The warning against deception acknowledges that they would hear plausible voices insisting these things were normal or harmless. Paul's reply: the standard is not social acceptability but the will of God, and God's wrath settles the matter regardless of what the culture permits.

7.synthesis: Therefore do not become partners with them. The verse is brief but the logic is clear: because the wrath of God falls on the sons of disobedience for these things, believers must not become their partners or companions in the vices that provoke that wrath. Sharing in someone's conduct means sharing in their standing before God. Clarke connects this directly to the warning of verse 6, urging the Ephesians not to act as their fellow citizens do or allow plausible philosophy to lead them astray from the path of truth. Barnes states the command simply: since these things displease God and expose to his wrath, avoid them. The brevity of the verse underscores how direct the inference is—no elaboration needed; the conclusion follows immediately from the premise.

voices.html: Do not act as your fellow citizens do. Do not suffer their empty and illusory doctrines to lead you astray from the path of truth. To become a partner in someone's conduct is to share their standing before God—and the sons of disobedience stand under divine wrath. That should be reason enough to maintain a clear separation from the practices Paul has enumerated.

Since these things displease God and expose those who practice them to his wrath, believers must avoid them. Partnership in conduct becomes partnership in consequence. Paul does not need to elaborate the point; the logic is as immediate as the danger. The community of believers exists to be something different from what surrounds it—a people who live by a different standard and reflect a different glory.

8.synthesis:
Verse 8 states one of the most arresting formulations in Ephesians: you were at one time darkness, but now you are light in the Lord. Paul does not say they were in darkness or walking in darkness—he says they were darkness. The identification is ontological: their entire existence, before the gospel reached them, was darkness in itself. Now they are light—not merely illuminated from outside but transformed in their nature. Ellicott notes this expression is unique and far more emphatic than the more common phrases of being in darkness or walking in light; it names a change of being, not merely of location or behavior.
