Calvin explains that the greater weight of what follows comes precisely from this contrast: having spoken of unbelievers as darkness, he can now argue with full force that believers must not become partakers of their crimes. Clarke notes that in their former heathen state they served divers lusts and pleasures, but now they belong to the Lord and carry his nature. Wesley puts it simply: they were once total blindness and ignorance, and they are now to walk as children of light—as people whose nature corresponds to what they have been made.

voices.html: Ye were sometimes darkness, but now are ye light in the Lord. This expression is unique and far more emphatic than the more common phrases of being in darkness or in light. Paul does not say they walked in darkness and now walk in light; he says they were darkness and now are light. The change is ontological—a transformation of being, not merely a change of environment or behavior.

The precepts that follow derive their weight from this contrast. Having spoken of unbelievers and warned believers not to share their crimes, Paul argues from what believers now are. They are no longer merely people who know better; they are light in the Lord—transformed in their nature so that they carry within them the quality of the one who said I am the light of the world. Light by nature cannot cohabit with the works of darkness.

They were themselves once darkness—not merely in darkness but darkness itself. The meaning is that their whole former manner of life and thought belonged to the category of darkness. Now, because they are in the Lord, they are light. The implication is immediate: light must act according to its nature—it must shine, it must expose, it must resist the dark.

9.synthesis: Verse 9 describes the fruit produced by the light: goodness, righteousness, and truth. There is a significant textual variant here: the majority of early manuscripts and ancient translations read fruit of the light (φωτός), while the received text familiar to later readers has fruit of the Spirit (πνεύματος). Ellicott argues strongly that fruit of the light is the correct reading, both on external grounds (manuscript evidence) and internal grounds (it maintains the coherence of the passage—light has its fruits just as darkness has its unfruitful works). Calvin notes the divergence without attempting to resolve it definitively in a brief note. Clarke cites the manuscript evidence for light as supported by most critical editors. Barnes accepts fruit of the Spirit as the reading but notes the dispute. Either reading carries the same essential meaning: the transformed life that flows from belonging to Christ produces these three qualities—goodness (moral excellence), righteousness (right relationship with God and neighbor), and truth (integrity of mind and word).

voices.html: The true reading is fruit of the light, for which the easier phrase fruit of the Spirit has been substituted, to the great detriment of the coherency of the passage. Light has its fruits; darkness has its unfruitful works. The contrast is deliberate and the logic requires the light-reading: just as the works of darkness produce no benefit, so the fruit of the light is genuine—goodness, righteousness, and truth.

Instead of Spirit, the majority of ancient manuscripts and early fathers read light, which is supported by most critical editors. The sense is either way the same: the transformed person, whether described as walking in light or as filled with the Spirit, produces goodness, righteousness, and truth—virtues that correspond to and reflect the divine character that has been imparted to them.

The Holy Spirit, through the gospel, produces goodness, righteousness, and truth—see that you exhibit these in your lives, and thus show that you are children of light. Whatever reading one accepts, the practical point is identical: a life genuinely transformed by God will be characterized by these three qualities, and their presence or absence is the test of whether the transformation is real.

9.key_tension: The manuscript tradition divides between 'fruit of the light' and 'fruit of the Spirit'; Ellicott and Clarke prefer 'light' on textual grounds; Barnes accepts 'Spirit' while acknowledging the dispute.

10.synthesis:
And try to discern what is pleasing to the Lord. This brief verse supplies the method by which the fruit of the light is cultivated: active, ongoing testing and discernment of what pleases God. The Christian life is not reduced to following a fixed list of rules but requires the exercise of renewed judgment in specific situations. The verb proving (δοκιμάζοντες) implies bringing each case to the full light of God, testing it, and approving what survives the test.

Ellicott connects this directly to Romans 12:2, where the same verb appears: the renewal of the mind transforms one's capacity to prove what is the good and perfect will of God. Clarke explains that walking in the light—under the influence of the divine Spirit and according to the dictates of the gospel—enables the believer to bring to full proof what pleases God. Barnes notes that the participle may be used instead of an imperative: the command is to walk as children of light in such a way that the very walk is a continuous process of discernment, testing each step against the standard of what God approves.

voices.html: The same word is used in Romans 12:2, where the renewing of the mind produces the capacity to prove what is the good and acceptable and perfect will of God. To prove is to try in each case, by the full light of God, what is pleasing to him. It implies a disciplined, active engagement of the renewed mind with every situation—not passive rule-following but living discernment.

By walking in the light, under the influence of the divine Spirit and according to the dictates of the gospel, believers are able to try and bring to full proof what pleases God. The darkness of their former life made discernment impossible; the light of Christ makes it not only possible but natural. The fruit of the light includes this capacity for spiritual perception that the unregenerate mind entirely lacks.

Walk as children of light, showing what is acceptable to the Lord. The participle implies not a single act but a continuous process: every step of the Christian life is an exercise in proving and discerning what God approves. This transforms the ethical life from rule-keeping to relationship—the question is not merely what is permitted? but what pleases my Father?

11.synthesis:
The positive call to bear the fruit of light (vv.8-10) has a negative counterpart: take no part in the unfruitful works of darkness, but instead expose them. The believer's relation to the works of darkness is not neutrality but active exposure. Light does not merely avoid darkness—it illuminates it. The verb reprove or expose (ἐλέγχω) carries the force of conviction by demonstration: showing darkness to be what it is, by the contrast of one's own life and by direct word when occasion demands.

Ellicott sharpens the meaning of have no fellowship with: it does not merely mean refusing to participate (which should go without saying) but refusing to keep terms with the works of darkness, having no sympathy or indulgence or excuse for them. Calvin notes that as children of light dwelling among a perverse and crooked generation, there is good reason to keep themselves apart from wicked actions and to use the advantage they have in the truth to reprove what is evil. Clarke connects this specifically to the heathen mystery religions, whose night rites produced exactly the darkness Paul has in mind.

voices.html: As children of light dwelling in the midst of a perverse generation, believers must keep themselves apart from wicked actions. But Paul goes further: they are to expose and reprove the works of darkness. Light does not merely refrain from darkness; it illuminates it. The Christian's life and word together perform the function of light—making visible what darkness conceals and showing sin for what it is.

To have no fellowship with the works of darkness does not merely mean refusing to participate in them—that should go without saying. It means keeping no terms with them, having no sympathy, no indulgence, no excuse for them. The believer is not to be a neutral observer of evil but an active exposer of it—a light that shows darkness what it is by the sheer contrast of its own character.

The unfruitful works of darkness produce no benefit to body or soul. Believers are not only to avoid them but to expose them—to make them visible for what they are. This is done primarily through the quality of one's own life, which stands as a constant contrast to the surrounding darkness, and also through direct reproof when love and wisdom call for it.

12.synthesis:
For it is shameful even to speak of the things that they do in secret. Paul justifies the command to expose rather than describe: the very act of detailed description would pollute the speaker. Sin may be plainly indicated and effectively condemned without the tongue being soiled by reciting its specific developments. The mention of things done in secret points to the clandestine character of the worst vice—the fact that it must be hidden is itself evidence that it is known to be wrong.

Ellicott notes that this caution is only too obvious when one reads ancient accounts of Greco-Roman religious and social practices. Calvin observes the proverbial truth: night has no shame, and what is concealed from human eyes is exposed to the eyes of God. Clarke is more specific, connecting the verse to the Eleusinian and Bacchanalian mysteries, which were performed in darkness and were so impure and abominable that even the ancients recognized it was shameful to describe them publicly. Barnes notes that missionaries of his own era could confirm the same principle—the worst of pagan practices remain too vile to describe in print.

voices.html: Paul shows the advantage of reproving the ungodly: if they can escape the eyes of men, there is no crime, however shocking, that they will not perpetrate. Night has no shame. What is concealed from human eyes they think is also hidden from God. But the very fact that these things must be done in secret condemns them—and the light that exposes them is both a reproach to evil and a mercy to those who might otherwise remain trapped in it.

This no doubt refers to the Eleusinian and Bacchanalian mysteries, which were performed in the night and darkness, and were known to be so impure and abominable—especially the latter—that even antiquity recognized it was a shame to speak of them. The darkness under which they were practiced was both a literal condition and a moral symbol: these were things that required concealment because they could not bear the light of public examination.

It is still a shame to speak of the practices of the heathen. Missionaries report that they cannot describe what they encounter in detail, so vile are the things done in secret. Paul's point is that the Christian approach to evil is not lurid exposure but principled contrast: let the quality of the believer's life be the condemnation, not a recitation of specific sins that would only pollute the speaker.

13.synthesis:
Verse 13 states the principle underlying the command to expose rather than participate: when anything is exposed by the light, it becomes visible, for anything that becomes visible is light. Light has two effects that are actually one: it makes things visible, and in making them visible it transforms them. The thing exposed by light does not remain darkness; it enters the domain of the visible, of the known, of the potentially transformed. This is the spiritual logic of reproof: the exposure of sin by the gospel is not merely condemnation but the beginning of rescue.

Ellicott translates the verse more accurately as when reproved, things are illuminated by the light—the emphasis falling on the illuminating action rather than the passive becoming-manifest. Calvin notes the grammatical ambiguity (middle or passive voice) but observes that the practical result is the same: light is the agent of making things known, and in that knowing lies the possibility of transformation. Wesley states the epistemological point simply: nothing but light from heaven can make anything manifest—the exposure of sin requires a source of illumination that the sinner cannot produce for himself.

voices.html: The proper rendering is when reproved, things are illuminated by the light. The emphasis is on illumination as an action performed by the light. When the works of darkness are exposed by the gospel, they are not merely named as evil; they are brought into the full visibility of truth, and in that visibility they are stripped of the power that concealment gave them. Light does more than condemn; it liberates.

All reprovable actions are made manifest as sinful by the gospel light. Whatever is revealed as evil by the light is thereby drawn out of the darkness that protected it. The practical consequence is that believers who live as light perform a service even for those whose sins they expose: the exposure is the beginning of the path from darkness to light, just as the gospel first exposes sin before it offers forgiveness.

All things which are reproved are thereby dragged out into the light, and made manifest—shown in their proper colors by the light. Nothing but light, yea, light from heaven, can make anything truly manifest. The sinner cannot see his sin clearly enough to repent of it until the light of God's truth falls on it. The reproof that exposes is therefore an act of grace as much as an act of judgment.

14.synthesis:
Paul introduces a quotation with the formula therefore he (or it) says—the same form used in Ephesians 4:8 for scriptural quotations—but the words that follow (Awake, O sleeper, and arise from the dead, and Christ will shine on you) do not correspond exactly to any Old Testament passage. Calvin, Ellicott, Clarke, and Barnes all survey the debate: possible sources include Isaiah 26:19, Isaiah 60:1, or a conflation of several passages. The most likely explanation, favored by Ellicott and Clarke, is that Paul is quoting a very early Christian hymn or liturgical formula—possibly a baptismal call—based on Old Testament imagery but expressing the new creation theology of the gospel.

The content of the call matches the movement of the whole passage: awakening from the sleep of spiritual ignorance, rising from the death of sin, receiving light from Christ himself. Wesley reads it as God's word to all who are still in darkness: awake, arise, and Christ—not the law, not human effort—will supply the light. Calvin sees Christ speaking through his ministers, issuing the call to spiritual resurrection that only the gospel can produce. Whatever its precise origin, the quotation functions as an invitation embedded in the argument: the light that has been described throughout this section is not merely a status or a duty but a person who actively calls the sleeping and the dead to rise.

voices.html: Paul exhibits Christ as speaking by his ministers: the call to awake and arise is the call of the gospel itself. Whatever the textual source, the content is clear—the light of Christ breaks into human darkness and calls the sleeping dead to rise. This is not merely a moral exhortation but a creative word, like the original let there be light, performing what it commands in those who receive it.

This formula is used in Ephesians 4:8 to introduce a scriptural quotation, but the words that follow are not found verbatim in any Old Testament passage. The most probable explanation is that Paul quotes an early Christian hymn or liturgical formula—possibly used at baptism—based on Isaiah 26:19 and 60:1 but expressing the resurrection theology of the new covenant. What matters is the content: awake from sin, rise from the dead, and Christ himself will be your light.

God says, in the general tenor of his word, to all who are still in darkness: Awake, thou that sleepest—in ignorance of God and thyself; in stupid insensibility. And arise from the dead—from the death of sin. And Christ shall give thee light—knowledge, holiness, happiness. The promise is not that effort will be rewarded but that Christ himself will illumine those who turn toward him.

It is a matter of debate and controversy whence this saying is derived—some trace it to Isaiah 26:19, others to Isaiah 60:1, others to a lost prophetic book. Most likely it is an ancient Christian hymn, perhaps used at baptism, drawing on several Old Testament images to express the radical transformation of new birth: the sleeper becomes awake, the dead arise, and the darkness is replaced by the light of Christ.

14.key_tension: The commentators agree on the meaning and function of the quotation but divide on its source—Calvin sees it as Christ speaking through ministers, Ellicott and Clarke identify it as an early Christian baptismal hymn, Wesley treats it as a general summary of God's call throughout Scripture.

15.synthesis:
Look carefully then how you walk, not as unwise but as wise. Having established the identity of believers as light and called them to expose darkness, Paul now addresses the quality of the walk itself. Circumspectly or accurately (ἀκριβῶς) is the word: the walk of the wise person is careful, precise, attentive to where each step falls. The contrast with the unwise person is not merely intellectual—the unwise person does not think where he is going, or does not make the best of his way.

Ellicott stresses that the word rendered circumspectly is properly strictly or accurately—generally used of intellectual accuracy or thoroughness. The sense is not anxious caution but disciplined precision, the same quality one applies to careful thinking applied instead to the whole conduct of life. Calvin notes that because everything around believers tends to corrupt and mislead—they walk among many thorns—such care is especially necessary. Clarke explains the word circumspect through its Latin root: to look around on all sides, to be every way watchful and cautious in order to avoid danger and discern enemies before they strike. Wesley captures the positive edge: exactly, with the utmost accuracy, reaching toward the highest pitch of every point of holiness.

voices.html: Everything around believers tends to corrupt and mislead, so that it is difficult for godly persons, who walk among so many thorns, to escape unhurt. Such corruption having infected the whole world, there is great reason for walking with care. Wisdom here is not cleverness but the disciplined attention to one's path that refuses to be tripped by the dangers that surround the Christian life on every side.

The word rendered circumspectly is properly strictly or accurately—the same word used of intellectual accuracy or thoroughness. The sense is not nervous anxiety but disciplined precision: applying to one's whole life the care a scholar applies to a text, or a craftsman to fine work. This is wisdom in action—not merely knowing what is right but executing it with rigor and care.

Walk circumspectly—exactly, with the utmost accuracy, aiming at the highest pitch of every point of holiness. Not as fools who think not where they are going, or who do not make the best of their way. The wise person treats every moment as a resource and every step as a decision with consequences, and walks accordingly.

16.synthesis:
Making the best use of the time, because the days are evil. The phrase redeeming the time (ἐξαγοραζόμενοι τὸν καιρόν) is borrowed from the commercial sphere: buying up the opportunity, purchasing the moment from competing claims. The word for time here is kairos—not clock time (chronos) but opportunity, the moment ripe for action. The reason given intensifies the urgency: because the days are evil. The ambient culture is hostile to what is good; time not used for the kingdom is readily captured by something else.

Ellicott notes the same phrase in Daniel 2:8 used negatively—to gain the time as a delaying tactic—which illuminates the positive meaning here: instead of wasting or delaying, the wise person buys back every opportunity for good. Calvin explains that Paul enforces the exhortation by the consideration of time: the corruption of the age means every moment either serves the gospel or is surrendered to darkness. Clarke reads the command as buying up the moments others throw away—steadily improving every present moment. Wesley states it with characteristic precision: saving all you can for the best purposes, buying every possible moment out of the hands of sin and Satan, sloth, ease, pleasure, and worldly business.

voices.html: By a consideration of the time Paul enforces his exhortation. Everything around tends to corrupt and mislead; there is corruption in the very air of the age. Because the days are evil—dominated by forces hostile to the gospel—every moment either serves the kingdom or is surrendered to the powers of darkness. The wise person wastes nothing; the fool allows time to drift through his fingers.

Buying up those moments which others seem to throw away. Steadily improving every present moment, so that you may, in some measure, regain the time already lost. Let time be your principal object. The days are evil—surrounding culture is hostile to the good—so every unguarded moment is time that is lost to the enemy. This is not anxious hoarding of time but active, purposeful engagement with every opportunity for good.

With all possible care, redeem the time—saving all you can for the best purposes; buying every possible moment out of the hands of sin and Satan; out of the hands of sloth, ease, pleasure, and worldly business—the more diligently because the present are evil days, days of the greatest difficulty and danger, in which the surrounding culture actively opposes the work of the Spirit.

17.synthesis:
Therefore do not be foolish, but understand what the will of the Lord is. The connection is explicit: the wise person who redeems the time (v.16) does so because he understands what the Lord wills in each moment and acts accordingly. Foolishness here (ἄφρων) is stronger than the more general word for lack of wisdom in verse 15; it is the word for senselessness, the practical irrationality of a person who acts without reference to the fundamental realities of his situation. The fool is not unintelligent but unrealistic—he behaves as if God's will is irrelevant to daily life.

Clarke draws a vivid connection to the Bacchanalian mysteries, whose votaries acted literally like madmen, running about and tossing their heads. This is the image of senselessness Paul is using: the person who orders his life without reference to God's will is as irrational and dangerous as a person in a religious frenzy. Ellicott notes the deliberate escalation from verse 15's word for unwise to this stronger word for senseless or foolish. Barnes focuses on the positive instruction: show true wisdom by both understanding what the will of the Lord is and doing it—knowledge and action together constitute the wisdom being commended.

voices.html: The person whose delight is in the law of the Lord, and who meditates in it day and night, will triumph over every obstacle Satan places in his path. Whence comes it that so many wander or fall? Because they do not meditate on the will of God. To understand the will of the Lord is the foundation of wise living; without it the most active and energetic person is simply rushing in the wrong direction.

Do not become madmen. There is a most evident allusion here to the orgies of Bacchus, in which votaries acted like madmen—running about, tossing their heads, and behaving as if entirely out of their senses. This is Paul's image of what it looks like to live without understanding the will of God: not peaceful ignorance but frantic, purposeless activity that achieves nothing for the kingdom.

Be not fools in the employment of your time and manner of life. Show true wisdom by endeavoring to understand what the will of the Lord is, and then doing it. The two halves belong together: understanding without action is still foolishness, and action without understanding is senselessness. The wise person takes the time to know what God wills and then commits himself fully to doing it.

18.synthesis:
And do not get drunk with wine, for that is debauchery, but be filled with the Spirit. The contrast is dramatic and deliberate. Wine and the Spirit are not merely opposite intoxicants; they represent two entirely different orientations of the self. Drunkenness is the pursuit of excitement through loss of self-control, an escape from reality into disinhibition. Being filled with the Spirit is the pursuit of fullness through surrender to the living God, an entry into reality more complete than sober selfconsciousness alone can provide.

Clarke and Barnes both connect the verse explicitly to the Bacchanalian mysteries, in which worshippers got drunk as an act of religious devotion, entering an ecstatic state they attributed to divine possession. Paul's implicit argument is that the Spirit offers what Bacchus falsely promised: genuine joy, genuine transcendence, genuine power—without the excess, moral dissolution, and morning-after emptiness. Calvin explains that be filled with the Spirit refers to the fullness of spiritual gifts and graces, not a single moment of enthusiasm. Wesley states the principle cleanly: the Spirit gives a more noble pleasure than wine can, and does so without the debauchery that wine in excess always produces.

voices.html: Drunkenness is here characterized not primarily as a physical appetite but as a reckless pursuit of excitement at all costs. Paul passes from general reckless levity to this special sin. The contrast with being filled with the Spirit is exact: drunkenness seeks stimulus through loss of control; the Spirit fills precisely by taking control—the result being not dissolution but power, not debauchery but holiness.

This is a further allusion to the Bacchanalian mysteries, in which votaries got drunk and ran into all manner of excesses. The pagans pursued ecstasy through intoxication; Paul offers the Spirit as the true and lasting ecstasy. Being filled with the Spirit produces genuine joy, genuine power, and genuine community—everything the mystery religions promised but could not deliver, without the morning-after wreckage that always followed wine-inspired excess.

Being filled with the Spirit, in all his graces, gives a more noble pleasure than wine can do. The person filled with the Spirit experiences a fullness of joy, peace, and power that no external substance can approximate. The contrast is not merely between a bad habit and a good one but between a counterfeit of the divine and the divine itself—between what the flesh offers and what God actually gives.

19.synthesis:
The fruits of being filled with the Spirit are described in verses 19-21. First: addressing one another in psalms and hymns and spiritual songs, singing and making melody to the Lord with your heart. The Spirit-filled community expresses itself in song—not merely in private devotion but in mutual address. The three categories (psalms, hymns, spiritual songs) are distinguished by the commentators with varying precision, but all agree they represent the full range of corporate sung worship, from the inherited psalter to new compositions prompted by the Spirit.

Clarke suggests psalms may refer to David's psalter, hymns to extemporaneous praise, and spiritual songs to any sacred composition on a divine subject. Wesley draws the same distinction, adding that because inspired songs specifically adapted to the Christian dispensation were not yet fixed, Christians were not limited to any one collection but were to sing from the fullness of the Spirit. Ellicott notes that in the parallel Colossians 3:16 the purpose is teaching and admonishing one another, while here the idea is more broadly a natural vent for the joy and gratitude that fill the Spirit-filled heart. Barnes observes that Paul links singing to edification: speaking to one another in songs implies that praise has a horizontal as well as a vertical dimension—it builds up the community as well as ascending to God.

voices.html: These are truly pleasant and delightful fruits of being filled with the Spirit. The singing Paul describes is an expression of joy in the Holy Ghost—a mutual edification that takes the form of song. He does not command the Ephesians to sing in public only but to make melody in their hearts to the Lord: the outer expression and the inner reality must correspond, or the singing is mere performance without life.

In the parallel passage in Colossians 3:16 the purpose is teaching and admonishing one another; here it is simply a natural vent for the joy of the Spirit-filled heart. The two are not contradictory: genuine worship teaches even when instruction is not its explicit aim, and the song that rises from a full heart carries its own persuasive power. Singing is named as both an expression of the Spirit's fullness and an instrument of mutual edification.

Speaking to yourselves in psalms—endeavoring to edify one another and to promote purity of heart by songs of praise. From the beginning, praise was an important part of Christian worship. These three categories—psalms, hymns, spiritual songs—together represent the full range of what believers may bring to worship: the inherited Word, spontaneous praise, and Spirit-prompted song of every kind.

20.synthesis:
Giving thanks always and for everything to God the Father in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ. The scope of thanksgiving here is uncompromising: always and for all things. This is not a standard achievable by natural optimism or favorable circumstances. It is the fruit of a heart that has been so transformed by the knowledge of God's sovereign goodness that it can find reason for gratitude even in adversity. The access point is the name of our Lord Jesus Christ—thanksgiving that flows through the mediator, sustained by the certainty that all things are under his Lordship.

Calvin explains that God's innumerable benefits always yield fresh cause for joy and thanksgiving, and that this is a pleasure which ought never to lose its relish—an exercise of which we ought never to weary. Ellicott connects this to 1 Thessalonians 5:18, where the same comprehensive thankfulness is described as indissolubly united with unceasing joy and prayer. Wesley notes the breadth: for all things, prosperous or adverse, since all work together for good. Barnes sees this verse as the conclusion of the section on Spirit-filled life that began in verse 18: the fullness of the Spirit issues in praise, song, and this encompassing attitude of gratitude that transforms the whole of life into an act of worship.

voices.html: The innumerable benefits received from God yield fresh cause of joy and thanksgiving. This is a pleasure which ought never to lose its relish and an exercise of which we ought never to weary. Paul means that thanksgiving is not an occasional duty but the permanent atmosphere of the Spirit-filled life—a posture of heart that finds God's goodness even in what is difficult because it trusts in the sovereign purpose behind all things.

This temper of universal and pervading thankfulness is dwelt upon in 1 Thessalonians 5:18 as indissolubly united with unceasing joy and prayer. The three—rejoice always, pray without ceasing, give thanks in everything—together describe the Spirit-filled life as Paul understands it: not a series of separate practices but a single integrated orientation of the heart toward God in every moment and circumstance.

Giving thanks for all things, prosperous or adverse, since all work together for good. In the name of our Lord Jesus Christ—by whom we receive all good things. The name of Christ is not a formula appended to prayer but the ground of access: it is through him that we may bring our thanksgiving to the Father, and through him that we may trust the Father's goodness even when the outward circumstances are dark.

21.synthesis:
Submitting to one another out of reverence for Christ. This transitional verse is both the culmination of the Spirit-filled life described in verses 18-20 and the introduction to the household code that follows in verses 22-6:9. Mutual submission is not a separate topic but the practical expression of the Spirit's work in community: a heart filled with gratitude (v.20) and reverence for Christ naturally yields its claims to others. The motivation is crucial: submission is not a social convention or a capitulation to power but a voluntary act flowing from the fear (reverence, awe) of Christ.

Calvin explains that God has bound us so strongly to each other that no one ought to try to avoid subjection, and where love reigns mutual service is rendered—he does not except even kings and governors, whose authority is held for the service of those beneath them. Ellicott notes the grammatical connection to the preceding verses: submitting is a participle that flows from being filled with the Spirit, meaning mutual submission is a sign and fruit of the Spirit's presence. Clarke observes that no one should be so tenacious of his own will or opinion in matters indifferent as to disturb the peace of the community—in all such matters, love should rule, yielding to one another. Barnes connects it to the specific relationships that follow: mutual submission is the general principle of which the submission of wives, children, and servants are particular instances.

voices.html: God has bound us so strongly to each other that no one ought to try to avoid subjection; where love reigns, mutual services will be rendered. Paul does not except even kings and governors, whose very authority is held for the service of those beneath them. Mutual submission is not social weakness but the proper posture of those who understand that they belong to one another as members of a body under a common Lord.

In grammatical construction this clause is connected with the preceding verses describing Spirit-filled life; in point of idea it leads on to the household code that follows. Submission is not a sudden new topic but the practical and specific expression of the Spirit's work: the person filled with the Spirit, singing and giving thanks, naturally submits to others in the reverence of Christ that pervades his whole orientation.

This general principle of religion Paul now proceeds to illustrate in reference to wives, children, and servants. Mutual submission is the foundation from which all specific subordination flows. The key word is reverence for Christ: submission among believers is not grounded in social hierarchy but in shared awe before the Lord who himself came not to be served but to serve—and who calls his people to imitate him.

22.synthesis:
Wives, submit to your own husbands, as to the Lord. Paul now applies the principle of mutual submission (v.21) to the most intimate of human relationships. The qualifier as to the Lord does not equate the husband with the Lord but establishes the motivation: a wife's submission is an act of Christian obedience to God's ordering of creation, not merely a social convention or a concession to male strength. This grounds the command in theology rather than culture, which has both a stabilizing and a limiting effect—stabilizing because it calls for genuine, whole-hearted submission; limiting because anything the Lord himself would not command falls outside its scope.

Calvin explains that besides the universal bond of subjection (v.21), some relationships carry additional specific obligations. Wesley notes that inferiors are placed before superiors throughout this section because the general proposition is about submission, and inferiors are to do their duty regardless of what their superiors do—a principle that protects the command from being made conditional on the husband's worthiness. Clarke clarifies that submission in everything lawful is required; the husband may be profligate or command what is wrong, and in such cases no submission is owed. Barnes sets out the background from 1 Corinthians 11, where the ordering of husband and wife is traced to creation rather than culture.

voices.html: Besides the universal bond of mutual subjection, some relationships carry additional specific obligations. Paul now addresses the marriage relationship: the wife is to submit to her husband as to the Lord. This is not submission to personality but to an office—a God-given ordering in which the wife's willing deference is itself an act of reverence toward the God who established the order, not merely toward the man who holds it.

Inferiors are placed before superiors throughout this household code because the general proposition is about submission, and inferiors are to do their duty whatever their superiors do. The command to wives is not conditional on the husband's worthiness or conduct. It flows from the wife's relationship to the Lord, and that relationship is not abrogated by a difficult or undeserving husband.

The duty of the wife's submission is grounded in creation—the husband is the head of the wife—and in the analogy to Christ and the church. It is not grounded in the husband's personal superiority in wisdom or character. The motivation is theological: submission to God's ordering in marriage is an expression of submission to God himself. This gives the command both its seriousness and its proper limits.

23.synthesis:
The reason for the wife's submission is stated: for the husband is the head of the wife even as Christ is the head of the church, his body, and is himself its Savior. The analogy to Christ and the church is the theological foundation of the household code—not merely an illustration drawn from an independently established social arrangement but the ontological reality on which the arrangement is grounded. Just as Christ's headship over the church is inseparable from his role as its Savior and Sustainer, so the husband's headship is to be understood in terms of responsibility and care rather than mere authority.

Ellicott observes that the comparison differs significantly from 1 Corinthians 11:3, where the head of every man is Christ and the head of the woman is the man, but the head of Christ is God—a chain of mediated authority. Here, the direct comparison is between husband-and-wife and Christ-and-church, which means the husband's headship is defined by the character of Christ's headship—self-giving, saving, sustaining. Calvin notes that the comparison ought to produce a stronger impression on both parties: it elevates the seriousness of the obligation and defines its character simultaneously. Clarke stresses that Christ exercises authority over the church so as to save and protect it, which is precisely what Paul goes on to require of the husband.

voices.html: Christ has appointed the same kind of relation to exist between husband and wife as between himself and his church. This comparison ought to produce a stronger impression on both parties than any other argument could. It defines what headship means: not the authority of a master over a servant, but the headship of a Savior who gives himself entirely for the body he heads and whose authority is inseparable from his self-giving love.

The comparison here is instructive when set beside 1 Corinthians 11:3. There, the head of every man is Christ and the head of Christ is God—a chain of authority. Here, the comparison is direct: husband is to wife as Christ is to church, which means the character of the headship is defined by Christ's own headship over the church—a headship that expresses itself in saving, sustaining, and ultimately self-giving love rather than in mere command.

The husband is the head of the wife as God ordained it. As Christ exercises authority over the church so as to save and protect it—not to exploit or oppress it—so the husband is to exercise his authority for the benefit and flourishing of his wife. The savior of the body language makes this explicit: the husband's role is protective and nurturing, not merely dominant.

24.synthesis:
Now as the church submits to Christ, so also wives should submit in everything to their husbands. The analogy established in verse 23 is now applied directly. The church's submission to Christ is the model; the wife's submission to her husband is its earthly reflection. The phrase in everything is qualified by every commentator who treats it: it means in everything that is lawful and consistent with the prior claims of God.

Calvin explains the nevertheless or but at the verse's opening as possibly anticipating an objection—Christ has a unique claim as Savior that no husband can replicate. Yet even acknowledging that unique claim, wives are still to submit as the church submits to its Lord. Clarke is direct: the submission is in every lawful thing; it is not intimated that wives should obey their husbands in anything criminal or anything detrimental to their souls. Wesley's compressed note states the same limit: in everything which is not contrary to any command of God. Barnes cross-references Ephesians 1:23 to show that the church's submission to Christ is the pattern for this and for all the relationships in the household code—a submission that is voluntary, loving, and oriented toward the head's stated purposes.

voices.html: Though Christ has a unique claim as Savior that no husband can replicate, the analogy holds for the purpose of defining the wife's posture. The church submits to Christ in everything that Christ commands; the wife submits to her husband in everything that God's law permits. The submission is real and substantial—not merely nominal—but it is bounded by the higher claim of the Lord to whom both husband and wife ultimately belong.

In every thing means every lawful thing. It is not intimated that wives should obey their husbands in anything criminal or detrimental to the interests of their souls. The husband may be profligate or command what is wrong; in such cases no submission is owed. The submission Paul requires is the voluntary, loving deference of an equal to an office—not the passive compliance of one who has surrendered all moral agency.

The church submits to Christ in all that pertains to its life and mission. The wife submits to her husband in the same spirit—in everything that falls within the proper domain of his headship, which is bounded by the prior and overriding claim of Christ. Submission to the husband is not an alternative to submission to Christ but a particular expression of it.

25.synthesis:
Having addressed wives, Paul turns to husbands—and the measure he sets before them is startling. Wives are told to submit; husbands are told to love their wives as Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her. The standard for the husband is not authority but sacrifice. The command to love (ἀγαπᾶτε) refers to the deliberate, costly, self-giving love Paul defined at the opening of the chapter (v.2)—the love that gave itself as a fragrant offering. Christ's love for the church is defined by self-giving, not self-assertion; by serving, not commanding; by dying, not demanding.

Calvin observes that husbands are required to cherish toward their wives no ordinary love—they are to honor them with the kind of love Christ has shown the church. Ellicott stresses that Christ's love for the church means he counts himself incomplete without her (Ephesians 1:23), bears with her weakness, and raises her to be one with himself—this is the measure of conjugal love. Clarke is direct: the husband is to lay down his life for his wife if necessary and to count nothing too difficult to promote her welfare. Barnes observes that the apostle would guard against the abuse of authority by enjoining on the husband a spirit so unlike domination that obedience on the wife's part becomes a natural response rather than a compelled one.

voices.html: From husbands the apostle requires that they cherish toward their wives no ordinary love; to them also he holds out the example of Christ. If they are honored to bear Christ's image in their relation to their wives, they are to bear it fully—including the self-giving, the sacrifice, the refusal to count the cost when the welfare of the one loved is at stake. The example of Christ makes extraordinary demands on husbands.

The love of Christ for his church is such that he counts himself incomplete without her, raises her to be one with himself, bears with her weakness and frailty, and gave himself entirely for her. This is the model for conjugal love. The husband is not called to command but to love with this quality of love—complete, patient, sacrificial, and focused entirely on the good of the one loved.

The apostle guards against the abuse of the husband's right by enjoining the manifestation of such a spirit that obedience on the wife's part becomes natural. The husband's duty is not authority but love—the same love that led Christ to give himself entirely for the church. Such love, properly exercised, creates the conditions in which willing submission is not a burden but a joy.

26.synthesis:
Christ gave himself up for the church, Paul continues, that he might sanctify her, having cleansed her by the washing of water with the word. The verse describes the means and goal of Christ's self-giving: the goal is sanctification, the means is cleansing through the washing of water connected to the word. There is an unresolved debate about whether the washing of water refers specifically to baptism or more broadly to the cleansing effect of the gospel word.

Ellicott is confident that the reference to baptism is unquestionable: the phrase laver of the water is the standard term for the baptismal pool in both Jewish (the laver of the tabernacle) and early Christian usage. Wesley interprets the washing as baptism, specifically if the outward visible sign is accompanied by the inward spiritual grace. Calvin reads the verse more broadly: the cleansing is accomplished by the forgiveness of sins and the regeneration of the Spirit, with the word (the gospel) as the primary instrument. Clarke notes that Christ washes, cleanses, and sanctifies the church through the word of the gospel, with baptism as the outward sign of the inward reality. Barnes accepts the baptism reference but emphasizes the word as the agent: water alone accomplishes nothing; it is the word of God that effects the cleansing of which baptism is the visible sign.

voices.html: The reference to baptism in the laver of the water is even more unquestionable than in the parallel passage in Titus 3:5. The phrase uses the standard term for the baptismal pool. The point is that Christ's self-giving for the church issues in a concrete act of cleansing through a means he has ordained—water and the word together, neither without the other, producing the sanctification that is his purpose for the church.

The cleansing is accomplished by the forgiveness of sins and the regeneration of the Spirit. Paul here mentions the inward and hidden sanctification and the outward and visible sign together. The word is the primary instrument—it is the gospel that cleanses and sanctifies—and the washing of water (baptism) is the outward sign that seals and confirms what the word accomplishes inwardly in the heart of the believer.

The great object of the Redeemer was to purify and save the church. The husband is to manifest a similar love toward his wife and a similar desire that she should walk before him in holiness. The cleansing that Christ effects through the washing of water and the word involves both the outward sign and the inward reality—the word of God as the cleansing agent and baptism as the appointed seal of what the word accomplishes.

26.key_tension: Ellicott and Wesley identify 'the washing of water' specifically with baptism; Calvin reads it more broadly as the word of the gospel, with baptism as the outward sign; Barnes accepts the baptism reference while emphasizing the word as the effective agent.

27.synthesis:
The purpose of Christ's cleansing extends to the eschatological goal: so that he might present the church to himself in splendor, without spot or wrinkle or any such thing, that she might be holy and without blemish. The imagery shifts to the wedding day—specifically the moment when the bride is presented to the groom in her full beauty. Ellicott notes the peculiarity that Christ presents the church to himself—both the one who prepares and the one who receives the gift.

Calvin explains that the design of baptism and cleansing is that believers may live in a holy and unblamable manner before God: we are washed by Christ not that we may return to our pollution but that we may retain throughout our lives the purity we have received. The eschatological dimension is important: the presentation in splendor is the final-day completion of what is already underway in sanctification. Wesley sees this as something Paul envisions even in this world—the church being made holy now as well as ultimately. Clarke connects the image to Psalm 45 (the king's daughter clothed in splendor) as the Old Testament backdrop. Barnes focuses on the bridal image: the church prepared to be presented to Christ as his spouse, without any defilement or defect that would be inappropriate in that ultimate union.

voices.html: The design of our being washed is that we may live in holy and unblamable manner before God. We are washed by Christ not that we may return to our pollution but that we may retain throughout our lives the purity we have received. The goal of all Christ's work—his self-giving, his cleansing, his ongoing intercession—is this final presentation: the church standing before him without spot, wrinkle, or any defect.

The original is emphatic: that he might Himself present her to himself. Christ is both the one who prepares the bride and the one to whom she is presented—a double action that underscores that the entire process, from cleansing to presentation, is the work of Christ alone. The bride brings nothing to the wedding except the beauty he has given her; the splendor is entirely his gift and his achievement.

Christ presents the church to himself—even in this world—as his spouse: all glorious within, not having spot of impurity from any sin, nor wrinkle of deformity from any decay. This is the goal already in process: sanctification is not merely a future state but an ongoing work, and the husband who loves his wife as Christ loves the church participates in this same work of bringing beauty from brokenness.

28.synthesis:
In the same way husbands should love their wives as their own bodies. He who loves his wife loves himself. Having expounded the Christ-and-church analogy at length, Paul now brings the application back to the husband with an argument from nature itself. Every man by nature loves himself—specifically, every man nourishes and cherishes his own body. Since husband and wife are one flesh (the union established in Genesis and confirmed in verse 31), the husband who loves his wife is, in the most literal sense, loving himself.

Calvin explains the logic: no man can love himself without loving his wife, because she is part of him. To neglect or harm her is a form of self-destruction; to love and cherish her is the expression of a healthy self-love that extends to encompass the one who has become one flesh with him. Ellicott observes that Paul here returns from the glorious digression on Christ and the church to the single point where husband can imitate—a deep and unfailing love. Clarke notes the ontological grounding: woman is, properly speaking, a part of the man, taken from his side and therefore literally flesh of his flesh and bone of his bone. Barnes observes that Paul had been drawn off course by the Christ-and-church analogy and now returns to the practical instruction: husbands, love your wives as your own bodies, for they are one flesh with you.

voices.html: An argument is drawn from nature itself. Every man by nature loves himself. But no man can love himself without loving his wife, who is part of himself. The husband who does not love his wife does not love himself—for she is his own flesh. To cherish her is not a sacrifice to someone other than yourself but the natural overflow of a healthy self-love that understands what the marriage union has made the two of them.

From the glorious digression on Christ and the church, Paul comes back to the one point where the husband can and must imitate it: a deep and unfailing love. So refers to the previous verse describing Christ's love. The practical application is sober but warm: love your wife as you love your own body, nourish and cherish her as you do yourself—because she is yourself, in the most literal sense that Genesis 2 establishes.

The woman is, properly speaking, a part of the man. God made man male and female, and woman was taken out of man's side. She is therefore flesh of his flesh and bone of his bone. The husband who loves his wife loves what is most truly his own; the husband who neglects or harms her wounds himself. This is not sentiment but ontology—the marriage union is a real union that makes the two genuinely one.

29.synthesis:
For no one ever hated his own flesh, but nourishes and cherishes it, just as Christ does the church. The argument from natural self-love continues and is completed. The two verbs—nourish (ἐκτρέφει, to rear from childhood, to build up) and cherish (θάλπει, to warm, to keep tender)—capture the comprehensive care that natural self-love produces. Calvin uses this to prove that men are obligated by the demands of their own nature to love their wives: the drive to care for oneself is so strong that it must extend, in a properly ordered man, to the wife who is one flesh with him.

Ellicott notes that these two verbs together describe the two fundamental aspects of natural care: building up (nourishment) and warming (cherishing). Wesley compresses the point cleanly: nourishing and cherishing the body means feeding and clothing it—the basic necessities of care. Barnes applies this to the marriage relationship: just as no man disregards the happiness and welfare of his own body, so he is obligated to show equal care for the wife who is one body with him. The verse closes by returning to the Christ-and-church analogy: Christ nourishes and cherishes the church with exactly this kind of comprehensive, sustaining care—and the husband's love for his wife is to reflect and participate in that divine pattern.

voices.html: Paul proceeds to enforce the obligations of marriage by representing to us Christ and his church. The strong affection a husband ought to cherish toward his wife is expressed in the fact that no man ever yet hated his own flesh. The same instinct that drives every healthy person to nourish and cherish his own body—the most universal and ineradicable human drive—is what must characterize a husband's love for his wife, who is literally his flesh.

He nourishes and cherishes his own flesh—that is, himself: feeds and clothes it. These verbs capture the two basic dimensions of care: building up by provision and warming by tenderness. Applied to marriage, they describe a love that both provides what is needed and maintains the warmth of intimate relationship—exactly the dual quality that Christ expresses toward the church he loves.

As no man disregards the happiness and welfare of his own body—his own self—so he should show equal care for his wife. She is one with him in the most fundamental sense Genesis establishes. The argument from natural self-love is therefore not merely an analogy but a direct inference: care for her is care for yourself, and the neglect of her is the self-inflicted harm of a man who does not understand what marriage has made him.

30.synthesis:
Because we are members of his body. The reason Christ nourishes and cherishes the church is stated: we are members of his body. The statement is ontological—it describes a real union, not a metaphor of association. As members of a body belong to the life of the head and receive from it their nourishment and direction, so believers are genuinely united to Christ in a way that makes his care for them inseparable from his own self-care.

Calvin stresses that Paul does not merely mean Christ is a partaker of our nature but expresses something higher: we share in Christ's very being, not merely in his qualities. Clarke explains: as Christ is the head of the church and the Savior of this body, and we are members of the church, we are therefore members of his mystical body. Wesley draws the parallel to Genesis that Paul is about to make explicit in verse 31: we are as intimately united to Christ in a spiritual sense as if we were literally his limbs. Barnes cites the extended treatment in 1 Corinthians 12 and John 15, where the same organic union is described in complementary imagery. The verse functions as the foundation for the quotation from Genesis that follows: the oneness of husband and wife in marriage is a type of the deeper oneness of Christ and the church.

voices.html: This is no exaggeration but the simple truth. Paul does not simply mean that Christ is a partaker of our nature but expresses something higher: we are members of his body in the fullest possible sense. The union is real, organic, and life-giving—not a polite fiction of association but the actual incorporation of believers into the living body of Christ, who nourishes and cherishes what is genuinely his own.

Christ has partaken of our nature as we have partaken of the nature of Adam. As he is the head of the church and the Savior of this body, and as we are members of the church, we are members of his mystical body. This is the ground of his care for us: we are not strangers he befriends but limbs he sustains because our life and his are genuinely one.

We are members of the body of Christ—genuinely united to him as limbs to a head. This organic union is described at length in 1 Corinthians 12 and John 15. Its application here is immediate: Christ nourishes and cherishes the church not from external benevolence but from the same motive that leads any living being to care for its own limbs. We belong to him, and he cares for what is his.

31.synthesis:
Paul quotes Genesis 2:24: Therefore a man shall leave his father and mother and hold fast to his wife, and the two shall become one flesh. In its original context this verse narrates the institution of marriage and explains its priority over even the family of origin. Paul has been working up to this quotation throughout the household code: the union of husband and wife is not merely a social arrangement but a divinely established oneness that serves as the type of the spiritual union between Christ and the church.

Calvin explains that as Eve was formed from the substance of her husband and was thus a part of himself, so those who are truly members of Christ are made one spirit with him—the marriage union and the believer-Christ union illuminate each other. Ellicott traces our Lord's own use of the same verse in Matthew 19:5 and notes the parallel structure between Paul's argument and Christ's teaching on the permanence of marriage. Clarke notes the verb rendered joined (προσκολληθήσεται—glued or cemented) suggests an adhesion stronger than almost anything else: as a well-glued joint may sooner break in the wood than at the joint, so death alone can part husband and wife. Barnes notes the phrase for this cause (answerable to what has been said) confirms that the Genesis citation is offered as the scriptural grounding for everything Paul has been arguing about the relationship of husband, wife, and Christ.

voices.html: As Eve was formed out of the substance of her husband and was thus literally a part of himself, so those who are truly members of Christ are made one spirit with him. Marriage is the divinely ordained type of that spiritual union. The Genesis verse does not merely state the origin of marriage but illuminates its inner meaning: two persons become genuinely one, as the church and Christ are genuinely one.

Our Lord quotes the same verse of Genesis in exactly the same way in Matthew 19:5. Paul's use of it here confirms that the marriage union is both a natural reality and a spiritual type: the oneness of husband and wife reflects and anticipates the oneness of Christ and the church. The Genesis verse is therefore not merely ancient history but the deepest theological statement about what every marriage is and means.

The Greek verb rendered joined means glued or cemented. A well-glued board will sooner break in the wood than at the joint; so death alone can part the husband and wife. The marriage union is meant to be as strong as the union between Christ and the church—indissoluble by human will, persisting through all the difficulties and disappointments of life because it rests not on feeling but on a covenant as permanent as the covenant between Christ and his people.

32.synthesis:
This mystery is profound, and I am saying that it refers to Christ and the church. Paul now names what has been implicit throughout the entire section: the mystery (mystērion) he has been unfolding is not primarily a statement about human marriage but about the relationship of Christ and the church, for which marriage is the type and anticipation. The Greek word mysterion in Pauline usage means a divine secret that has now been revealed—a hidden truth disclosed in the gospel.

The Latin Vulgate rendered mystērion as sacramentum, and on this basis the Roman Catholic Church has historically listed matrimony among its seven sacraments. Calvin dismisses this interpretation as resting on a mistranslation and a misreading: the mystery Paul calls great is the union of Christ and the church, not the institution of human marriage. Clarke traces the controversy in detail, noting that the Vulgate's sacramentum has supported a tradition unsupported by the Greek text. Barnes is equally direct: the original conveys no sense of a sacrament; it simply names a profound divine truth that has been revealed. The great mystery is Christ's relationship to the church—the deepest reality that human marriage points toward without being able to fully contain.

voices.html: Paul expresses his astonishment at the spiritual union between Christ and the church. By mystery he means that no language can fully explain what it implies. But the mystery he calls great is the union of Christ and the church, not the institution of human marriage. Those who have inferred from the Vulgate's sacramentum that marriage is a sacrament have built a vast ecclesiastical tradition on a translation error.

This mystery is great—so the Greek reads, not this is a great sacrament as the Vulgate has it. On the evidence of the Latin version, the Church of Rome has made matrimony a sacrament. But the mystery Paul calls great is not the marriage union but the spiritual union between Christ and the church, for which marriage serves as the earthly type and appointed sign.

The original here conveys no such sense as sacrament. The word means a profound divine secret now disclosed in the gospel. The great mystery is Christ's love for the church—his intimate union with her, his self-giving for her, his ongoing care for her—which human marriage is designed to represent and reflect. Paul's concern is not to elevate marriage as a rite but to reveal what the institution of marriage has always been pointing toward.

32.key_tension: The Vulgate's rendering of mystērion as sacramentum formed the basis for the Roman Catholic teaching that marriage is a sacrament; Calvin, Clarke, and Barnes all argue that this rests on a mistranslation, and that the great mystery Paul names is the union of Christ and the church, not the marriage institution itself.

33.synthesis:
Having digressed into the profound mystery of Christ and the church, Paul returns with a brief practical summary: however, let each one of you love his wife as himself, and let the wife see that she respects her husband. The verse is a compressed recap of the entire household code as it applies to marriage. Husbands are summarized by love; wives by respect (φοβῆται—literally, fear or reverence). The verb for the wife's duty is notably different from the verb used in verse 22 (submit); here it is a matter of deep regard, of holding the husband in honor.

Calvin notes that having digressed from the subject, Paul adopts the method of short precepts and gives a brief summary: love is the husband's fundamental obligation; reverence is the wife's. Both are addressed to the will, not merely to external conduct. Ellicott observes that the primary and perfect application of the Christ-and-church analogy belongs to Christ alone; but the teaching is to be applied practically in marriage: husband loves, wife reverences. Clarke grounds both duties in the divinely ordained design of marriage: love because she is naturally and by divine ordinance a part of him, reverence because he is her head by the same divine ordinance. Barnes closes the practical instruction by noting that love and reverence are complementary: the husband's love creates the conditions for the wife's reverence, and the wife's genuine honor for her husband completes and encourages his love.

voices.html: Having digressed, Paul returns to short precepts and gives a brief summary of duties. Husbands are required to love their wives; wives to reverence their husbands. Both duties are addressed to the will: love is not merely a feeling but a commitment of the self to the good of another, and reverence is not merely politeness but genuine honor for the person who holds the office of head in the marriage union.

Although the primary and perfect application of the Christ-and-church analogy belongs to Christ alone, the teaching is to be applied practically in marriage: let the husband love his wife as himself, and let the wife reverence her husband. The word reverence is properly fear—the same deep regard that marks a right relationship to God—here applied to the wife's attitude toward the husband who holds his office by divine appointment.

Let every husband love his wife as himself, because she is both naturally and by divine ordinance a part of himself. And let the wife reverence her husband—render him proper respect as the head of the family and the representative of Christ in the domestic order. Each duty is grounded in God's design for marriage, not in the personal merits of the individual who holds the office.
